ib_brokerage_runtime.description: SRS-EXE-006 IB Gateway brokerage adapter. The DEFAULT build ships the fully-tested deterministic half: IB-error -> SyRS SYS-64 classification (classify_ib_order_error -> brokerage_error -> AdapterError::Brokerage, unblocking SRS-ERR-001's broker-validation categories INVALID_SYMBOL/INSUFFICIENT_BUYING_POWER/RATE_LIMITED/CONNECTIVITY_BLOCKED), the TWS transport seam, and the four AC operations exposed through the CANONICAL BrokerageAdapter/MarketDataAdapter/HistoricalDataAdapter traits (SYS-52) so failures flow through the common AdapterError taxonomy (proven solo via a FakeIbGateway). Config fails closed on malformed/non-Unicode ATP_IB_* values and non-literal-IP hosts (IbConnectionConfigError). The live socket transport (TcpIbGateway) is behind the NON-DEFAULT ib-live-transport feature so the default public surface never advertises a half-built live path. STAYS passes:false (serialized): the AC's automated IB paper-account integration test is operator-initiated (SyRS SYS-2e, fixed port 4002, --features ib-live-transport) and cannot run in the parallel agent pool.
deferred: operator-initiated IB paper-account integration test (SyRS SYS-2e / AC-2, --features ib-live-transport): completes the TWS wire encoding in TcpIbGateway and runs submit/cancel/subscribe/historical green against port 4002 (flips passes:true)
execution-engine wiring of the IB adapter to atp_execution::LiveBrokerageSubmit (SRS-EXE-001 / SRS-ORCH-*)
IB TWS API version-upgrade regression testing against the paper account (SRS-EXE-007 / SYS-65)

error_handling_contract.description: The structured order-error envelope (StructuredOrderError: category + error_type + message + original_order) and the SyRS SYS-64 OrderErrorCategory vocabulary are built in atp-types. The err001_error_envelope_cli operator binary (the cli sub-block) is a Phase-1 surface that drives EVERY currently-implemented production StructuredOrderError construction site for failed order submissions: the single-live authority gate (ExecutionEngine::route_order, NotDesignated), the inner mode/connectivity/freshness gate (ExecutionEngine::submit_live_order — paper non-live, IB unreachable, scheduled-restart, stale market data), and the idempotency ledger (OrderLedger::submit — duplicate client correlation id, the SRS-EXE-008 rejection specified 'per SRS-ERR-001'). categories proves the SyRS SYS-64 vocabulary is total + stable; envelope proves every implemented reject path returns a complete category + type + message + unchanged-original-order envelope; no-broker proves a rejected order reaches no brokerage; authority drives route_order through the engine-owned live-designation registry to prove a non-designated strategy is rejected (no broker) while the single designated live strategy is authorized (SRS-EXE-001 single-live invariant). SRS-ERR-001 stays passes:false: SYS-64 (docs/SyRS_v0.7.md) names broker-side order-validation error types — INVALID_SYMBOL, INSUFFICIENT_BUYING_POWER, RATE_LIMITED — that are vocabulary-only today (no production construction site) and require the deferred IB adapter (SRS-EXE-006) to be produced and contract-tested before SRS-ERR-001 can flip to passes:true.

deferred.what: INVALID_SYMBOL / INSUFFICIENT_BUYING_POWER / RATE_LIMITED broker-side order-validation envelopes (deferred IB adapter) — the blocking deferral keeping SRS-ERR-001 passes:false
SUBSCRIPTION_LIMIT_REACHED rejections carry the same envelope but are raised by the market-data subscription manager
INGESTION_RECORD_VALIDATION_FAILED / INGESTION_PACING_BUDGET_EXCEEDED categories are raised by the data layer, not as order submissions, and do not construct StructuredOrderError
HOT_SWAP_DEMOTION_TIMEOUT / KILL_SWITCH_LIQUIDATION_TIMEOUT and the deployment/resource categories are owned by the orchestrator and kill-switch gates

sequence_gap_contract.description: SRS-MD-007 (the market-data subscription manager shall detect sequence gaps in IB tick streams and reflect gap state in heartbeat/staleness; SyRS SYS-39 / SYS-39a / SYS-70; NFR-P5; StRS SN-2.03 / SN-2.04). SCOPE: this contract pins the IN-PROCESS gap-detection + per-security staleness half. MarketDataTick.tick_seq is the UPSTREAM PROVIDER sequence (see tick_sequence_source) that also serves as the SRS-MD-001 fan-out correlation id; SRS-MD-001 deferred its gap semantics to MD-007, which defines them here (a lost upstream tick leaves a skip the detector reads as a gap -- so the ingestion adapter MUST supply the provider origin sequence, not a delivery-renumbered counter). SequenceGapDetector (crates/atp-market-data) tracks, per canonical SecurityKey, the last observed tick sequence and the resulting MarketDataFreshness of that security's consolidated upstream line: a forward skip (observed > last + 1) is a GAP -- it publishes a SequenceGapEvent (symbol, expected_sequence, observed_sequence, observed_at_ns) through the SequenceGapEventSink port and marks the line Stale; a fresh monotonic tick (observed == last + 1) or an operator-acknowledged resync (acknowledge_resync) RECOVERS the line to Fresh (the two SRS-MD-007 recovery conditions); a duplicate / backwards tick (observed <= last) is neither a gap nor a recovery and leaves staleness unchanged. freshness() fails CLOSED: a security never observed returns Stale so an order on a silent line is blocked. The MarketDataFreshness the detector reports is the SAME atp-types enum the SRS-MD-004 execution gate (ExecutionEngine::submit_live_order via the MarketDataFreshnessProbe port; freshness_contract) rejects MARKET_DATA_STALE on for live AND paper submissions -- so the gap -> stale -> order-block chain is closed at the type level in-process. Wiring the detector to that gate is the deferred orchestrator-layer runtime adapter (atp-execution must not depend on atp-market-data per SRS-ARCH-002), and it must close a real seam gap: the CURRENT MarketDataFreshnessProbe is SYMBOL-ONLY (freshness(&self, symbol: &str)) while the detector is keyed on the full SecurityKey (symbol + asset class). Making the port security-aware -- carrying the asset_class OrderSubmission already holds, or a SecurityKey -- is a deferred change on the SRS-MD-004 / ERR-3 port surface (see deferred[]); until then an equity and an option sharing a display ticker cannot be distinguished by the symbol-only probe, but the detector FAILS CLOSED on options (never tracked -> Stale) and options are rejected upstream, so equities (the only currently-tradable class) are unaffected. The gap event is shaped to feed the SRS-LOG-001 LogRecord seam (Source.MARKET_DATA, event_type SEQUENCE_GAP, pinned in log_record_contract.event_types_by_source and python/atp_logging). SRS-MD-007 stays passes:false in feature_list.json until the deferred runtime + operator surfaces land (see deferred[]).

gap_event_sink_port.rationale: SRS-MD-007 makes logging a first-class AC ('gap events ARE logged via SRS-LOG-001 ... visible on the dashboard'), so a durable-write / transport failure must be SURFACEABLE, not silently swallowed. The failure is reported on GapObservation::Gap.published (an Err), NOT by aborting the observation -- a gap that could not be logged is still a detected gap and the line is still stale. The SAFETY outcome is publication-independent: observe_tick commits Stale BEFORE record, so a failing sink still fails CLOSED (line stays stale, orders blocked). Durable fail-closed persistence + operator page are the concrete SRS-LOG-001 / SRS-NOTIF-001 sink's job.

detector.stale_onset_note: stale_since_ns is set ONLY on the Fresh->Stale transition (guarded by `if was_fresh`), so a repeated gap on an already-stale line does NOT reset the staleness age reported on the heartbeat/dashboard.

tick_sequence_source.meaning: UPSTREAM provider sequence (the value the ingestion adapter reads from IB's own feed), monotonic per consolidated security line; doubles as the fan-out correlation id.
tick_sequence_source.producer_contract: The detector finds a gap as a SKIP in tick_seq, so gap detection is ONLY meaningful if tick_seq carries the upstream provider origin sequence -- a lost upstream tick must leave a hole. A counter re-numbered 1,2,3,... per DELIVERED callback would be gap-free by construction and silently defeat detection. Populating tick_seq from the provider origin sequence is the ingestion adapter's obligation (deferred SRS-EXE-006 / feed loop). SRS-MD-001 deliberately deferred tick_seq's gap semantics to SRS-MD-007, which defines them on MarketDataTick::tick_seq. Pinned in-process by producer_contract_a_delivery_renumbered_stream_hides_gaps.

md_004_gate.port_signature_today: freshness(&self, symbol: &str) -> MarketDataFreshness (SYMBOL-ONLY; the detector is SecurityKey-keyed, so faithfully bridging needs the port to carry asset_class / SecurityKey -- deferred, see deferred[])

deferred.what: The async IB tick-ingestion feed loop that calls observe_tick for each delivered tick with a real wall-clock timestamp, and the wall clock itself. SequenceGapDetector is single-threaded and clock-free (observed_at_ns is injected). CRITICAL producer obligation: this ingestion adapter MUST populate MarketDataTick.tick_seq with the UPSTREAM provider origin sequence (so a dropped IB tick leaves a skip the detector can find), NOT a delivery-renumbered counter -- see tick_sequence_source. Gap detection is only as sound as this producer contract; without a real IB feed supplying a gap-bearing sequence, MD-007 cannot be verified end to end, which is why it stays passes:false. The runtime that wraps the detector + the SRS-EXE-006 IB adapter that delivers the (gap-bearing) ticks flip SRS-MD-007 passes:true together.

The concrete MarketDataFreshnessProbe adapter (orchestrator layer) that reads freshness()/stale_since_ns() so submit_live_order blocks MARKET_DATA_STALE on a gap-stale line. The block itself is already built + tested in atp-execution (freshness_contract / err_3_stale_data_blocked); the detector produces the exact MarketDataFreshness::Stale it consumes. atp-execution must not depend on atp-market-data (SRS-ARCH-002), so the bridge lives in the higher orchestrator layer with the feed loop.

PREREQUISITE for a faithful bridge: MarketDataFreshnessProbe is SYMBOL-ONLY today (freshness(&self, symbol: &str) / staleness_seconds(&self, symbol: &str), called with submission.symbol in submit_live_order), but SequenceGapDetector is keyed on the full SecurityKey (symbol + asset class). A symbol-only probe cannot distinguish an equity from an option on a shared display ticker, so the port must become security-aware -- carry the asset_class OrderSubmission already holds, or a SecurityKey -- with the submit_live_order / dispatch_order call sites and every MarketDataFreshnessProbe stub (ERR-1/2/3, EXE-001/002 tests + err001 CLI) + freshness_check.py + freshness_contract updated in lockstep. Owned by the SRS-MD-004 / ERR-3 port surface, NOT by the MD-007 detector; the detector already fails closed on options (never tracked -> Stale) and options are rejected upstream, so equities (the only currently-tradable class) are safe in the interim. Raised by the /codex:adversarial-review round-1 [high]; deferred per scope (widening a passing shared port across ~16 sites of other features is out of scope for the detector).

The COMPANION time-based heartbeat freshness monitor. MD-007 owns SEQUENCE-gap staleness; MD-003 owns elapsed-time (>15 s no update) staleness. A runtime freshness view merges both into one MarketDataFreshness per line; stale_since_ns is the seam MD-003's clock reads to compute MarketDataFreshnessProbe::staleness_seconds for a gap-stale line.

The persistent system-log sink for SEQUENCE_GAP events (Source.MARKET_DATA) AND its fail-closed durability. SequenceGapEvent is shaped to feed the LogRecord seam and the event_type is pinned in log_record_contract + python/atp_logging. SequenceGapEventSink::record is FALLIBLE (Result<(), SequenceGapPublishError>): a durable-write / transport failure is SURFACED to the caller on GapObservation::Gap.published (an Err) so the runtime can fail closed + alert on lost audit evidence, rather than silently swallowing a required log. The SAFETY outcome is publication-independent: observe_tick commits the Stale state (the SRS-MD-004 order-block) BEFORE calling record, so a dropped/failed event still leaves the line stale (fail closed), never a silently-tradable gap (pinned by gap_stale_state_is_fail_closed_when_publication_fails). The concrete durable, retryable, fail-closed PERSISTENCE of the gap audit record (the atp_logging.persistence store already fsyncs + fails closed) and the operator page on a persistence failure are owned by the SRS-LOG-001 / SRS-NOTIF-001 runtime sink that implements this port, NOT the detector. Raised by the /codex:adversarial-review round-2/3 [high]; the concrete sink is deferred to the runtime that binds it, with the port shape (fallible) + safety-independence proven in-process.

The dashboard heartbeat / staleness pane and the REST/WebSocket HEARTBEAT-channel surfacing of per-line gap state ('visible on the dashboard'). The detector exposes the read accessors (freshness, is_stale, stale_since_ns); binding them to the dashboard/API is the deferred operator-interface half (the HEARTBEAT channel is a contract-only seam in asyncapi).

observe_tick fails closed on AssetClass::Option (SecurityKey::new returns OptionContractIdentityRequired -> SubscriptionRegistryError::OptionContractUnsupported) so distinct option contracts on one underlying never share a sequence stream. The full option contract identity that would let options be tracked is deferred to the features that own it, matching the subscription registry.

workload_event_sink_port.record_signature: fn record(&self, event: WorkloadAdmissionEvent) -> Result<(), WorkloadEventSinkError>

deferred: Concrete HostMemoryProbe backed by sysinfo / procfs / /proc/meminfo (owner: SRS-ARCH-004 + SRS-ORCH-003 — Phase 1 Proxmox VM target). Until that lands, the gate consumes the probe trait through stubs in tests; no real host memory is observed. The contract pin on `forbidden_imports_in_orchestrator_crate` prevents the orchestrator crate itself from depending on sysinfo — the concrete impl will live in a separate adapter crate.

Concrete WorkloadRegistry that lists active strategy containers, ingestion jobs, factor jobs, backtest runs, and Jupyter sessions (owner: SRS-ORCH-001 + SRS-ARCH-004 — same Docker driver entry deferred on the lifecycle contract). Until that lands, the registry is consumed through stubs.

Concrete WorkloadEventSink wired to the dashboard alert pane + SRS-LOG-001 audit trail + SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher. SRS-ORCH-003's acceptance criterion 'dashboard and notification alerts are emitted' is satisfied at the orchestrator boundary today — every refusal / eviction / probe-failure / registry-failure / termination-failure path issues a structured `WorkloadAdmissionEvent` through the typed port, and the 5 audit-event variants (Refused, Terminated, TerminationFailed, HostProbeFailed, RegistryListingFailed) discriminate the failure mode for the dashboard alert lane. Emission is best-effort by design: the gate calls `sink.record(...)` and does not abort or roll back the admission decision on a sink Err (the decision is irreversible once made). The typed `WorkloadEventSinkError` exists so a future caller wrapping the orchestrator can observe sink failures separately from admission outcomes without retrofitting the trait. Durable delivery semantics (bounded queue + retry, persistent journal, fan-out to a secondary channel) belong on the SRS-LOG-001 (audit log) + SRS-NOTIF-001 (notification dispatcher) + SyRS SYS-13 (dashboard) implementations — same deferred dispatcher block that consumes HealthCheckEventSink in the orchestrator-lifecycle contract.

Concrete failure variants on WorkloadTerminationError, HostMemoryProbeError, and WorkloadRegistryError. The gate already consumes all three port methods as Result<..., Error> and refuses safely on any failure (probe error → refuse; active() error → refuse; terminate() error → emit TerminationFailed + skip banking), but the error types today carry only a short reason string. Future variants — sysinfo refresh failed, /proc/meminfo unavailable, Docker engine timeout, cgroup permission denied, registry desynchronized, etc. — will be added when the concrete adapters land so the dashboard can render the specific failure cause.

Workload-agnostic admission envelope. SRS-ORCH-003 ships the SyRS SYS-57 hierarchy as a workload-agnostic data model (WorkloadPriority + WorkloadKind + RegisteredWorkload) but the admission gate currently accepts a StrategyLaunchRequest, which is the right shape for strategy admission (the orchestrator's primary domain — SRS-5.2). Non-strategy admission paths (ingestion-job, factor-pipeline-job, backtest-run, research-session) belong to their owning services and will introduce their own gate methods consuming the same WorkloadPriority library. The structural separation between request envelope and workload-priority is intentional, not drift — the priority parameter is decoupled from request.mode because SYS-57 ranks workloads categorically, not by strategy mode.

Production caller of StrategyOrchestrator::admit_workload — main.py is still a `pass` stub and no Rust orchestrator service binary invokes the gate. Owner: SRS-ARCH-001 production wiring (orchestrator service entry point).

Typed coupling of `admit_workload` to `launch`. Today the two gates are separate methods and `launch`'s Rustdoc states that calling it without first calling `admit_workload` bypasses the SyRS SYS-57 / SYS-58 enforcement. A future session will turn that 'must call' precondition into 'cannot fail to call' via a typestate (`admit_workload` returns an `AdmissionToken`; `launch` consumes the token) or a builder pattern. The typestate change is scoped to land when the production caller of `launch` is wired (same owner as the deferred entry above) so the parameter-list expansion happens once. Until then, the contract-level requirement is exercised by the integration + domain tests for SRS-ORCH-003 (codex critic: arch:admission-gate-bypass).

Gate-level capture and propagation of WorkloadEventSink::record errors. Today emission is best-effort (see WorkloadEventSink trait doc) — the gate does not capture or surface sink failures because the admission decision is irreversible once made. A future session will introduce a richer return shape (e.g. AdmissionOutcome { decision, sink_failures: Vec<...> } or a side-channel collector) once durable-delivery semantics are owned by the concrete SRS-NOTIF-001 dispatcher and gate-level retry / propagation becomes meaningful (codex critic: adapter:sink-error-discard).

strategy_api_scheduler_contract.description: Trading-calendar-aware scheduling primitives for SRS-SDK-002 (SyRS SYS-6 / SYS-50 / SYS-51; StRS SN-1.09 / SN-1.19 / BG-1 / BG-7).
strategy_api_scheduler_contract.calendar_horizon_pin_rationale: exchange_calendars.get_calendar without explicit start/end uses a host-date-rolling default window (~20y backward, ~1y forward). Pinning to a fixed [2000-01-01, 2035-12-31] window makes session-validity decisions date-deterministic across runs so the same historical or scheduled query does not silently flip from valid to CalendarHorizonExceeded as the wall clock advances. Widen the window when SRS-BT-* needs older data or live scheduling exceeds 2035.
deferred: Live wall-clock fire loop is owned by SRS-EXE-001 + SRS-ORCH-* (orchestrator dispatches scheduled callbacks; this module resolves fire times).
Pre-market and after-hours market-data subscription routing is owned by SRS-MD-* (market-data subscription manager).

CBOE Options is aliased to XNYS — exchange_calendars ships no dedicated CBOE Options calendar (XCBF is CBOE Futures with a 16:15 ET close, wrong asset class). NYSE / NASDAQ / CBOE-Options share the SIFMA U.S.-equity-class regular-session holiday calendar in practice for 2026; the alias is correct for the strategy SDK's equity-class option workflows. CBOE-specific option-only early closes (e.g., quad-witching schedule adjustments that diverge from NYSE) are deferred. Owner: future feature that ships maintained CBOE Options session tables. Update _EXCHANGE_HANDLES in python/atp_strategy/calendar.py and re-target the L7 documented-alias test when a real handle lands.

strategy_api_subscriptions_contract.description: Single tradable asset class invariant for SRS-SDK-003 (SyRS SYS-5 single-tradable-class + SyRS SYS-64 structured-error contract; StRS SN-1.07 / BG-1 / BG-5). A strategy configured for equities or options may subscribe to both asset classes for analysis; order submission against the non-configured class raises AssetClassViolation. Identical contract for live IB execution and internal paper simulation per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14.
deferred: MARKET_DATA_STALE and SUBSCRIPTION_LIMIT_REACHED structured-error types (per SyRS SYS-64) are owned by SRS-MD-002 (market-data line-limit enforcement) and SRS-MD-004 (stale-data order blocking); the SDK surface here exposes the asset-class guard only.

Live IB and internal paper simulation StrategyContext implementations are owned by SRS-EXE-001 / SRS-SIM-001 — they must call assert_asset_class before routing the order. The L7 domain test for SRS-SDK-003 ships a reference _RefStrategyContext that exercises this contract; production drivers re-use the SDK helper rather than reimplementing the comparison.

strategy_api_order_events_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for SRS-SDK-004 order event callback delivery (SyRS SYS-7 live-callback delivery + SYS-85 paper engine reuses the same IF-7 API surface + NFR-P4 p95 latency budgets; StRS SN-1.22 / SN-1.29 / BG-1). SCOPE: this contract covers the AC-required *field presence* half of SRS-SDK-004 ONLY. The NFR-P4 *latency proof* half (live <1,000 ms p95 from broker fill acknowledgement; paper <100 ms p95 from simulated fill) requires the real SRS-EXE-001 live dispatcher and SRS-SIM-001 paper dispatcher to exist and is gated on those features. Until then, SRS-SDK-004 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the SDK surface here is the prerequisite production dispatchers will consume. The SDK ships the OrderEvent / OrderEventType surface plus an assert_order_event_payload guard Python dispatchers must call before invoking Strategy.on_order_event so user code can rely on the documented field-presence contract; Rust core runtime dispatchers apply the same field-presence rules locally per the AGENTS.md one-way dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK). This metadata block is the cross-language source of truth — the Python SDK constants LIVE_CALLBACK_LATENCY_P95_MS / PAPER_CALLBACK_LATENCY_P95_MS are the Python-side view kept in parity with required_live_callback_latency_p95_ms / required_paper_callback_latency_p95_ms below, and Rust dispatchers read those same keys directly (or from a generated Rust constants file derived from them). The four AC-named callback categories (FILL, PARTIAL_FILL, CANCELLED, REJECTED) must include fill_price, fill_quantity, and commission per docs/SRS.md row SRS-SDK-004; dispatchers populate explicit zeros on a never-filled cancel or reject and cumulative average / total on a partially-filled cancel or reject. Live and paper modes use the same delivery surface per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14.
deferred: Live IB execution dispatcher (broker fill acknowledgement -> assert_order_event_payload -> Strategy.on_order_event) is owned by SRS-EXE-001. End-to-end live p95 latency proof requires a running IB Gateway and is verified by SRS-EXE-001's contract / performance tests rather than the SDK contract here.

Internal paper simulation dispatcher (simulated fill -> assert_order_event_payload -> Strategy.on_order_event) is owned by SRS-SIM-001. End-to-end paper p95 latency proof requires a running simulation engine and is verified by SRS-SIM-001's contract / performance tests.

The L7 domain test for SRS-SDK-004 ships a reference _RefDispatcher exercising both the field-presence guard and a deterministic synthetic in-process latency measurement under the PAPER budget; production dispatchers re-use the SDK helper and budget constants rather than redefining them.

strategy_api_warmup_contract.description: Warm-up mechanism for SRS-SDK-005 (SyRS SYS-8 warm-up + NFR-R3 restart re-execution; StRS SN-1.23 / SC-18 200-bar warm-up / BG-1 / BG-2 / BG-7). The SDK ships a WarmupController + WarmupState lifecycle + assert_warmup_complete guard that every concrete dispatcher (live IB execution per SRS-EXE-001, internal paper simulation per SRS-SIM-001, backtest engine per SRS-BT-001) must drive before delivering the first executable bar. The AC ordering rule (historical bars replayed via Strategy.on_bar before any executable bar, indicator buffers and rolling windows initialised before any trading signal can be generated, on_warmup_complete fired exactly once at the boundary) is enforced at the SDK surface. The controller is fail-closed on a short historical replay: if HistoricalData.get_bars returns fewer than warmup_bars bars for any subscribed symbol the controller raises WarmupNotComplete, leaves WarmupState at IN_PROGRESS, does not invoke on_warmup_complete, and keeps the executable gate (assert_warmup_complete) closed. Identical surface in live and paper modes per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime dispatchers re-implement the same gate locally per AGENTS.md dependency direction (no Rust -> Python SDK dependency).
strategy_api_warmup_contract.required_history_shortfall_behavior: raise WarmupNotComplete (StrategyAPIError); keep WarmupState at IN_PROGRESS; do not call Strategy.on_warmup_complete; keep assert_warmup_complete closed
deferred: Live IB execution dispatcher (live market-data stream gated on WarmupState.COMPLETE) is owned by SRS-EXE-001. The live dispatcher must construct WarmupController, invoke run() before subscribing to live executable bars, and call assert_warmup_complete at the executable boundary.
Internal paper simulation dispatcher (shared live market-data stream gated on WarmupState.COMPLETE) is owned by SRS-SIM-001. Mirror of the live path; the dispatcher must drive the same controller.

Backtest engine warm-up phase (historical replay window split between warm-up and evaluation regions) is owned by SRS-BT-001. Same controller drives the warm-up segment; executable bars begin after WarmupState.COMPLETE.

NFR-R3 restart re-execution of the warm-up mechanism is owned by SRS-EXE-005 (live persistence + restart) and SRS-SIM-004 (paper persistence + restart). On container restart the dispatcher constructs a fresh WarmupController and replays history; the SDK contract here is the same surface both restart paths consume.

The L7 domain test for SRS-SDK-005 ships a reference _RefDispatcher and _StubHistoricalData that exercise the AC end-to-end with a 200-bar warm-up + a 200-period SMA. The test proves: (a) all 200 historical bars hit on_bar before the first executable bar; (b) indicator buffers (SMA.is_ready) are True at the executable boundary; (c) order submission attempted during warm-up raises WarmupNotComplete; (d) on_warmup_complete fires exactly once.

strategy_api_indicators_contract.description: Built-in technical indicators for SRS-SDK-006 (SyRS SYS-35 + AC-6 + StRS SN-1.20 + C-9). The SDK ships SMA, EMA, RSI, MACD, BollingerBands, and ATR as thin glue wrappers around BOTH TA-Lib and pandas-ta, choosing the per-indicator canonical reference (see primary_backend_per_indicator: SMA and BollingerBands route through pandas-ta; EMA / RSI / MACD / ATR route through TA-Lib). SyRS AC-6 prohibits custom reimplementations of indicators available in these libraries; the wrappers contain buffering, dtype-conversion, and NaN -> None glue ONLY, with no arithmetic. Each indicator class implements atp_strategy.api.Indicator (value / is_ready / update). MACD additionally exposes MACDValue via .reading (macd, signal, histogram); BollingerBands exposes BollingerValue via .reading (middle, upper, lower). All six classes support incremental updates: a fresh Bar appended to an internal rolling deque triggers a recompute (TA-Lib for EMA/RSI/MACD/ATR; pandas-ta for SMA/BollingerBands) over the buffer and the wrapper exposes the last finite output. The L7 parity test additionally cross-checks every indicator against the OTHER library so AC-6 'wraps both' is verified end-to-end, not just at the import line. The same public surface is used in live IB execution (SRS-EXE-001), internal paper simulation (SRS-SIM-001), backtest (SRS-BT-001), and Jupyter research (SRS-RES-002) per SRS-SDK-001 AC-14. TA-Lib's C library is a mandatory native dependency (see docker/strategy-python.Dockerfile, docker/jupyter.Dockerfile, and .github/workflows/ci.yml install TA-Lib 0.6.4); the Python wrapper requires the C shared library on the loader path.
strategy_api_indicators_contract.primary_backend: mixed: pandas-ta for SMA / BollingerBands; TA-Lib for EMA / RSI / MACD / ATR
strategy_api_indicators_contract.parity_reference: the OTHER library — every indicator pins parity against both backends in the L7 test
strategy_api_indicators_contract.nan_to_none_convention: TA-Lib emits np.nan for warm-up positions where the indicator buffer is not yet full. Wrappers MUST convert the trailing NaN to Python None on .value and .reading so user strategy code does not need to special-case NaN. For compound indicators (MACD, BollingerBands) the whole reading is None if any component leg is NaN.
deferred: Extended indicator coverage (ADX, OBV, Stochastic, Ichimoku, etc.) is intentionally out of scope; SRS-SDK-006 names exactly SMA / EMA / RSI / MACD / BollingerBands / ATR. Future indicator additions follow the same wrap-talib-and-pandas-ta pattern enforced by this contract.
Strategy-author-facing documentation for the indicator surface (usage examples, edge-case guide, performance notes) is owned by SRS-SDK-009 (P1, docs).

Optimising for O(1) per-update cost is out of scope. The SRS-SDK-006 AC says 'support incremental updates', not 'O(1) per update'. O(period) recompute per bar via TA-Lib's C-level loop is acceptable and matches the prior pure-Python implementation's effective cost.

Documented seed-bar divergence between TA-Lib and pandas-ta on RSI / MACD / ATR (different Wilder/EMA seeding conventions) is not a bug. The L7 IndicatorPandasTaParityTest slices the first pandas_ta_seed_skip_bars_factor * period bars before comparing, and the divergence is documented in the indicators.py module docstring.

strategy_api_documentation_contract.description: User-facing documentation surface for SRS-SDK-009 (SyRS NFR-U2, StRS SN-1.01, C-1). The Python Strategy API ships with author-grade docstrings on every public name in atp_strategy.__all__, a README guide under python/atp_strategy/README.md that walks the full SDK surface, and a python/atp_strategy/examples/ subpackage containing self-contained runnable Strategy subclasses that demonstrate every documented capability end-to-end. The acceptance bar (per SRS-SDK-009 AC) is that a Python-proficient trader can author a new strategy using only the docstrings + README + example files, without ever needing to open python/atp_strategy/api.py or any other internal file. The contract block here is the cross-language source of truth for the tier system, README sections, and example modules; the evidence script tools/strategy_api_documentation_check.py and the L3/L7 tests under tests/test_strategy_api_documentation_contract.py and tests/domain/test_api_documentation.py enforce it.
anti_drift_rules: Every identifier referenced by a from atp_strategy import statement in any example module under python/atp_strategy/examples/ MUST appear in atp_strategy.__all__.

Every example module under python/atp_strategy/examples/ MUST import only from atp_strategy (the package facade), from atp_strategy.examples (sibling scaffolding such as the local _harness helper), or the Python standard library. Deep-path imports into platform internals (from atp_strategy.api import _x, from atp_strategy.warmup import _y, from atp_strategy.indicators import _z, from atp_strategy.scheduler import _w, from atp_strategy.calendar import _q) are forbidden because they tell strategy authors to read platform internals.
Every example module MUST define exactly one class that is a strict subclass of atp_strategy.Strategy.

No example module may contain conditional branches discriminating on the strings 'live', 'paper', or 'backtest' (case-insensitive) — SRS-SDK-001 AC-14 requires the same source to run in all three modes.
Every `python -m atp_strategy.examples.<name>` invocation referenced in python/atp_strategy/README.md MUST resolve to a module name in required_example_modules.

Every code fence (``>>> `` doctest or ```python``` fenced block) inside the docstring of any public symbol in atp_strategy.__all__ MUST import only from atp_strategy (the facade) and reference only names in __all__. Deep-path imports in docstring examples teach strategy authors that platform internals are the supported surface, contradicting the SRS-SDK-009 AC.

Every ```python``` code fence inside python/atp_strategy/README.md MUST import only from atp_strategy and reference only names in __all__. The same anti-internals rule applies to the README as to the docstrings.
deferred: Sphinx / mkdocs / readthedocs HTML rendering of the docstring + README surface is out of scope for SRS-SDK-009. The AC is satisfied by the in-source docstring + README + runnable examples surface; auto-rendered HTML can be added later without changing the SDK contract.

Doctest-style ``>>> `` execution via pytest --doctest-modules is intentionally NOT wired in. The author-facing convention chosen here is single-language ```python``` code fences in the README and example-runner verification in the L7 domain test; pytest --doctest-modules would constrain docstring style (mock everything, single-line scenes) without adding signal beyond the L7 example-runner.
Translation / internationalization of docstring content is out of scope; the platform is single-locale per StRS scope.

A formal style guide for future docstring additions (e.g., enforced Google-style or NumPy-style Args/Returns/Raises sections) is intentionally deferred. The contract here pins minimum docstring length per tier, a usable example fence on tier-1 names, and the runnable-example coverage rule — sufficient for the SRS-SDK-009 AC without forcing a particular section header format on the existing surface.

startup_readiness_gate_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for ERR-9 (Missing or invalid startup configuration: hold system in pre-trade state and expose readiness failure through logs, dashboard, and API). SCOPE: this contract covers the *gate state machine* + *structured payload* halves of ERR-9 only. (a) The pre-trade hold state machine that consumes the SRS-ARCH-005 validator output and gates live and paper order submission. (b) The structured payload shape that the downstream log sink (SRS-LOG-001), dashboard (SRS-UI-001), and REST/WebSocket API (SRS-API-001) read when those surfaces land. (c) The four-field operator-override audit-trail contract SRS-MD-006 requires for any manual release of the hold. The DEFERRED halves are: (1) the concrete log sink that persists as_log_records (SRS-LOG-001); (2) the dashboard pane rendering as_dashboard_payload (SRS-UI-001); (3) the live REST/WebSocket endpoint serving as_dashboard_payload (SRS-API-001); (4) the runtime readiness probes for IB connectivity, IB account authentication, SSD data layer access, ingestion freshness within one trading day, system service health, and NAS reachability (SRS-MD-006); (5) the persistent operator-override audit log + notification dispatch (SRS-LOG-001 + SRS-NOTIF-001). Until those land, ERR-9 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the SDK-surface here is the seam those features will plug into. Identical to the SDK-004 / SDK-005 partial-pass pattern. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime services that need to consult the pre-trade hold state (e.g., the orchestrator gating live order routing per SRS-EXE-001) re-implement the same gate locally per the AGENTS.md dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK). The Python module path and required exports below are the Python-side view; the cross-language gate_state_variants / allowed_transitions / forbidden_transitions / operator_override_required_fields keys are the contract Rust dispatchers consume directly (or from a generated Rust constants file derived from this block).
deferred: Concrete log sink that consumes as_log_records is owned by SRS-LOG-001. The SDK pins the JSON-line field set (timestamp_ns, severity, key, category, reason, srs_trace) so the persistent log surface can ingest without renegotiation when it lands; the sink itself, retention, and rotation policy belong to SRS-LOG-001.

Dashboard rendering of as_dashboard_payload (operations view + readiness pane) is owned by SRS-UI-001 / UI-1. The SDK pins the document field set (state, ok, errors, warnings, evidence, overrides) so the dashboard backend can render without renegotiation when it lands.

Live REST/WebSocket endpoint serving as_dashboard_payload (e.g., GET /api/v1/system/readiness) is owned by SRS-API-001 / API-2. The endpoint declaration in python/atp_api/routes.py already references readiness as a future capability; the body schema will be the dataclass-serialised dashboard payload defined here.

Runtime readiness probes for IB connectivity, IB account authentication, SSD data layer access, ingestion freshness within one trading day, system service health, and NAS reachability are owned by SRS-MD-006. The SDK contract here is the gate state machine those probes will write into; today the gate is seeded only by the SRS-ARCH-005 static-config half. SRS-MD-006 will extend reevaluate (or add a sibling assert_runtime_ready_or_hold) to fold the runtime-probe outcomes into the same state machine. ERR-9 cannot flip passes:true until both halves are wired.

Persistent operator-override audit log + SRS-NOTIF-001 alert dispatch on override issuance are owned by SRS-LOG-001 + SRS-NOTIF-001. The OperatorOverride dataclass + the four-field audit-trail contract are pinned here; the durable record keeping + the audit-event fan-out are downstream consumers.

Manual-override-mode escalation (e.g., dashboard banner indicating the system is running on an active operator override, time-to-live on overrides) is intentionally not part of the SDK contract. The gate exposes the override audit list so a future SRS-UI-001 / SRS-NOTIF-001 follow-up can implement the banner without renegotiation.

operator_workflow_surface_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for SRS-API-001 (expose dashboard, CLI, and REST/WebSocket interfaces for operator workflows). SCOPE: this contract is the cross-surface AC-coverage half of SRS-API-001 only. The three per-surface contracts (rest_api, cli, websocket_api) above already pin each surface in isolation; this block adds the SRS-API-001 AC binding that ties REST Capability + CLI Group + (optionally) WebSocket Channel buckets to the eight operator workflows the AC enumerates verbatim: 'Live designation, strategy management, kill switch, Hot-Swap, Reservoir ranking, backtests, system status, and logs are available through documented API paths or CLI commands.' Without this contract a CLI group rename, a REST route deletion, or a Capability/Group bucket renaming would not be caught by any existing test; the per-surface checks verify their own slice but not the SRS-API-001 cross-surface requirement. The DEFERRED halves (concrete request/response handlers, the HTTP server, the WebSocket server, the CLI runner dispatcher, and every downstream subsystem that the handlers will route to: SRS-EXE-001 / SRS-EXE-006 for kill-switch and live-designation IB sequencing, SRS-ORCH-004 / SRS-ORCH-005 for strategy lifecycle, SRS-RESV-002..006 for Hot-Swap and Reservoir ranking, SRS-BT-001 / SRS-BT-009 for backtests, SRS-DATA-002 for watchlist, SRS-LOG-001 for the logs sink, SRS-NOTIF-001 for alerts) are owned by the features named in the deferred[] list below. Until those land, SRS-API-001 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the SDK-surface here is the seam those features will plug into. Identical to the SDK-004 / SDK-005 / ERR-9 partial-pass pattern. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime services that need to consult the documented operator workflow set re-derive the same workflow IDs locally per the AGENTS.md dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK).
operator_workflow_surface_contract.ac_phrase_canonical: Live designation, strategy management, kill switch, Hot-Swap, Reservoir ranking, backtests, system status, and logs

deferred.what: Real kill-switch + live-designation route handlers + IB cancel / liquidate / halt / disconnect sequence. The SDK pins the route paths, request fields, response fields, and the requires_confirmation guard so the handler can land without renegotiation; the IB-side sequencing belongs to SRS-EXE-001.
Headless IB Gateway brokerage adapter the kill-switch / live-designation handlers route to. Until this lands the kill-switch and live-designation REST routes have no IB-side execution partner.

Strategy lifecycle dispatcher behind /api/v1/strategies and the `strategy` CLI group (start / stop / restart / rollback). The SDK pins the action vocabulary and the deployment_version_hash response field; the orchestrator wiring belongs to SRS-ORCH-004.

Strategy rollback target_version_hash resolution + live-rollback confirmation guard. The SDK pins requires_confirmation on the lifecycle rollback action; the orchestrator-side rollback semantics belong to SRS-ORCH-005.

Hot-Swap trigger handler + demotion-then-promotion sequencing behind /api/v1/hot-swap and the `hot-swap trigger` CLI command. The SDK pins requires_confirmation and the candidate_strategy_id request field; the Reservoir-side sequencing belongs to SRS-RESV-003.

Reservoir ranking query handler behind /api/v1/reservoir/ranking. The SDK pins eval_window_days, rankings, sharpe, sortino, and momentum_score field shapes; the ranking engine belongs to SRS-RESV-002.

Backtest launch handler behind /api/v1/backtests. The SDK pins strategy_id, start_date, end_date, parameter_overrides, cost_model, uploaded_parquet_id request fields and the backtest_id + queued_at response; the backtest runner belongs to SRS-BT-001.

Backtest results query handler behind GET /api/v1/backtests/{backtest_id}. The SDK pins the metrics + trade_log + equity_curve + benchmark_comparison response shape; the persistence layer belongs to SRS-BT-009.
Watchlist GET/PUT handlers behind /api/v1/watchlist. The SDK pins the symbols list and pacing_budget_remaining response field; the IB minute-bar pacing budget belongs to SRS-DATA-002.

Log query handler behind /api/v1/logs + the `admin logs` CLI command + the LOGS WebSocket channel publisher. The SDK pins the log_class / severity / source / event_type / correlation_id / start_time / end_time request fields and the events response shape; SRS-LOG-001 owns this end-to-end — the persistent sink, the system-log vs strategy-log separation, and registering the query handler / publisher on the now-built operator-interface-runtime registry (python/atp_runtime). The runtime owner map attributes the LOGS REST/CLI/WS operations to SRS-LOG-001 (the actionable blocker); the runtime only provides the registry/dispatch substrate.

Alert query handler behind /api/v1/alerts + the `admin alerts` CLI command + the ALERTS WebSocket channel publisher. The SDK pins the alert_id / raised_at / severity / channel / delivery_status / acknowledged response shape; the notification dispatch and delivery accounting belong to SRS-NOTIF-001.

Dashboard binding to the WebSocket channels (PNL, METRICS, ACCOUNT_STATUS, HEARTBEAT, STRATEGY_STATE, RESERVOIR_RANKING) and the REST surface. The SDK pins the channel set and refresh ceilings (NFR-P2 ≤5 s); the dashboard rendering belongs to SRS-UI-001.

BUILT in python/atp_runtime (stdlib HTTP + RFC6455 WebSocket + CLI dispatcher; verified by tools/operator_interface_runtime_check.py). The runtime now serves the full declared REST/WS/CLI surface on a loopback bind and enforces the interface invariants (SRS-SEC-002 loopback-only bind, UI-4/SRS-SAFE-001 confirmation guard, structured interface errors, SRS-SEC-001 secret redaction). What REMAINS deferred for SRS-API-001 to flip passes:true: the domain handlers behind each operation — they register on atp_runtime.HandlerRegistry as their owning features land (SRS-EXE-001 kill-switch/live-designation, SRS-ORCH-004/005 lifecycle/rollback, SRS-RESV-002/003 ranking/Hot-Swap, SRS-BT-001 backtest launch, SRS-DATA-002 watchlist, SRS-LOG-001 logs, SRS-NOTIF-001 alerts); until then those operations return a structured 501 naming the owner.

Domain readiness signal behind the `readiness wait` CLI command and the runtime's GET /api/v1/system/status report. The runtime owns its own liveness/status (ready=false while domain workflows are deferred), but the real trade-readiness gate — IB Gateway connectivity, IB account auth, SSD data-layer access, ingestion freshness within one trading day, system service health, NAS reachability — belongs to SRS-MD-006's startup readiness checks. `readiness wait` blocks on that real signal, so it stays deferred (501 / NOT_IMPLEMENTED) until SRS-MD-006 lands; the runtime owner map attributes it to SRS-MD-006.

log_record_contract.description: SDK-surface contract for SRS-LOG-001 (separate persistent system logs from user strategy logs; render each entry with timestamp / severity / source / event type / message / correlation ID; both classes viewable from the dashboard). SCOPE: this contract covers the *structured record schema* + *sink-routing boundary* halves of SRS-LOG-001 only. (a) The LogRecord dataclass + Severity / Source / LogClass StrEnums pin the six AC-named fields + the SRS-LOG-001 separation discriminant + the optional strategy_id that the strategy half requires. (b) The RoutedLogDispatcher + LogSink Protocol pin the seam SRS-LOG-001's persistent sinks plug into when they land; the dispatcher validates the schema at the dispatch boundary and routes on log_class so a SYSTEM record cannot land in the strategy sink or vice versa. (c) EVENT_TYPES_BY_SOURCE pins the SyRS SYS-61 / SRS-LOG-001 enumerations of system event types (order routing decisions/outcomes, ingestion start/completion/failure, container start/stop/restart/OOM-kill, IB Gateway connect/disconnect/reconnect, kill switch activations, Hot-Swap promotion/demotion, resource threshold alerts, market data subscription changes). The operator-log-store SUBSTRATE (durable JSON-Lines system/strategy stores, the system-vs-strategy physical separation, the queryable read surface, and the retention/rotation policy) is now BUILT in atp_logging.persistence (contract block: log_persistence_contract) for records dispatched in-process. It is a substrate, NOT a complete runtime half: what REMAINS deferred (and keeps SRS-LOG-001 at passes:false) is (a) the core-runtime event FORWARDING path — how the Rust-owned system-event producers (order routing, IB Gateway state, kill-switch activations, ingestion/container lifecycle) actually reach the operator store — so the persistent system log is not yet populated from the real core, only from in-process dispatches; (b) the dashboard log pane rendering owned by SRS-UI-001; (c) the live GET /api/v1/logs REST handler body + LOGS WebSocket channel publisher + admin logs CLI runner owned by SRS-API-001. Until those land, SRS-LOG-001 stays passes:false in feature_list.json; the schema/dispatcher here remain the SDK surface those features (and the now-built persistence substrate) plug into. Identical to the SDK-004 / SDK-005 / ERR-9 / SRS-API-001 partial-pass pattern. The cross-language source of truth lives here; Rust core runtime services that emit logs re-implement the same record locally per the AGENTS.md dependency direction (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK). The Python module path and required exports below are the Python-side view; the cross-language severity_variants / log_class_variants / source_variants / system_source_variants / event_types_by_source keys are the contract Rust emitters consume directly (or from a generated Rust constants file derived from this block).

deferred.what: BUILT — the concrete persistent sinks for the system + strategy halves (durable append-only JSON-Lines, system-vs-strategy separation enforced at the sink, opt-in size-based rotation + retention, and the queryable read surface GET /api/v1/logs consumes) now ship in atp_logging.persistence (contract: log_persistence_contract; check: tools/log_persistence_check.py). The SDK pinned the record schema, the LogSink Protocol, and the separation invariant; the operator-store substrate plugged in without renegotiation. SRS-LOG-001 still holds at passes:false because what remains is NOT the sink but: the core-runtime event FORWARDING path (Rust-owned producers — order routing, IB Gateway, kill-switch, ingestion/container lifecycle — do not yet write to the operator store, so the system log is populated only from in-process dispatches); the dashboard pane (SRS-UI-001); and the live REST/WebSocket/CLI handlers (SRS-API-001). See log_persistence_contract for the substrate + its deferred[].

Dashboard log pane rendering for both the system and strategy log classes (per the AC 'both log classes are viewable from the dashboard' half). The SDK pins the LogRecord.as_dict() shape so the dashboard backend can render without renegotiation; the rendering belongs to SRS-UI-001.

Live GET /api/v1/logs REST handler body + LOGS WebSocket channel publisher + admin logs CLI runner. The route / channel / command declarations already exist in python/atp_api/routes.py, python/atp_ws/channels.py, and python/atp_cli/commands.py (pinned by the operator_workflow_surface_contract block), and the operator-interface-runtime substrate (HTTP/WS/CLI dispatch + handler registry) is now BUILT in python/atp_runtime. SRS-LOG-001 registers the GET /api/v1/logs query handler, the LOGS WebSocket publisher, and the admin-logs CLI handler on that registry once its persistent sink lands — so SRS-LOG-001 owns these LOGS operations end-to-end (the runtime only provides the registry/dispatch substrate).

Notification dispatch when ERROR / CRITICAL records land (IB connectivity loss, kill-switch activation, resource alerts). The SDK pins the severity enum + the source enum so the notification subsystem can subscribe to a filtered stream without renegotiation; the dispatcher fan-out + email/SMS delivery belong to SRS-NOTIF-001.

Strategy-side logging API exposed within the Python Strategy API (e.g., self.log.info(...) inside on_data). The LogRecord schema + LogClass.STRATEGY half + Source.STRATEGY pinning are the seam this future SDK convenience helper plugs into; the helper itself belongs to a follow-up SRS-SDK feature.

The HTTP server + WebSocket server + CLI runner that bind the GET /api/v1/logs route, LOGS WebSocket channel, and admin logs CLI command to the persistent sinks. Same downstream owner as the SRS-API-001 deferral above; named here so the contract block enumerates every consumer of the SDK seam.

log_persistence_contract.description: RUNTIME-half contract for SRS-LOG-001 (separate persistent system logs from user strategy logs). This block pins the concrete persistent sinks that the SDK-surface log_record_contract deferred to 'SRS-LOG-001's runtime half'. atp_logging.persistence ships: (a) JsonlLogStore — a durable, append-only, JSON-Lines LogSink bound to exactly one LogClass; each store writes to its own physical file so SYSTEM and STRATEGY logs are persisted to SEPARATE sinks (the literal SRS-LOG-001 requirement), and the store ALSO refuses a wrong-class record at write() so a caller bypassing the dispatcher still cannot cross-contaminate the two audit trails. (b) build_separated_log_dispatcher — the boot wiring that binds a SYSTEM store and a separate STRATEGY store to one RoutedLogDispatcher. (c) read_records / JsonlLogStore.read / query — the read surface the live GET /api/v1/logs handler, the admin logs CLI runner, and the dashboard log pane consume; the query filters mirror the GET /api/v1/logs parameters (log_class / severity (minimum) / source / event_type / correlation_id / start_time / end_time). DURABILITY: every write appends one json line then flush + os.fsync (default on) so a kill-switch activation or IB disconnect survives a crash; a torn trailing fragment (crash mid-write, no terminating newline) is dropped — never fabricated into a record — and a complete-but-unparseable line fails closed with LogStoreCorruptionError rather than being silently skipped. ROTATION is opt-in (max_bytes=None default → unbounded append → no audit record is ever evicted); when set, at most max_files rotated segments are retained per the documented retention policy. SCOPE: a store is owned by a single writing process (in-process thread-safe via a lock; cross-process concurrent writers are out of scope). LANGUAGE/ARCHITECTURE: this sink is Python because it is the dashboard/API backend for the operator log surfaces (it backs the Python GET /api/v1/logs REST endpoint, the dashboard log pane, and the admin logs CLI). AGENTS.md permits the dashboard backend/API in another language 'if it does not become a core runtime service'; this is operator-facing read/display infrastructure, not part of the Rust trading core (execution/data/simulation). The Rust core emits its own log records locally per log_record_contract (Rust core must not depend on the Python SDK); a core-runtime durable sink + the Rust→operator-store forwarding path is a separate, deferred concern. DEFERRED (why SRS-LOG-001 stays passes:false): (a) the core-runtime event FORWARDING path — the Rust-owned system-event producers (order routing, IB Gateway state, kill-switch activations, ingestion/container lifecycle) do not yet write to this store, so the persistent system log is populated only from in-process dispatches, not the real core; (b) the dashboard log pane rendering (SRS-UI-001); (c) the live GET /api/v1/logs REST handler + LOGS WebSocket publisher + admin logs CLI runner (SRS-API-001) that register the query handler on the operator-interface-runtime registry. This block ships the operator-store SUBSTRATE, not a complete runtime half. The PASS line is 'SRS-LOG-001 PERSISTENCE PASS'.

The persistent system log is therefore populated only from in-process Python dispatches, not from the real Rust core. subscription_fanout_contract.description: SRS-MD-001 is the consolidation + fan-out half of SyRS SYS-70 (the line-limit half is SRS-MD-002 / subscription_limit_contract). This SDK-surface models the structural contract: ConsolidatedSubscriptionRegistry in atp-market-data owns the live subscription set, keeps exactly ONE upstream IB market-data subscription per distinct security regardless of how many strategy containers subscribe, fans received MarketDataTicks out to every subscriber of the tick symbol (and to no other subscriber), publishes a SubscriptionChangeEvent (SyRS SYS-61 / SRS-LOG-001 subscription_change, Source.MARKET_DATA) on every line-affecting / dedup transition, and serves as the concrete SubscriptionLineCounter the SRS-MD-002 limit gate consumes (closing subscription_limit_contract.deferred[]'s 'owner: SRS-MD-001' item). The consolidated set is keyed on a canonical SecurityKey (normalized symbol + AssetClass) so case/whitespace variants share one line; admission is ATOMIC — subscribe enforces the configured line ceiling in the same mutable borrow that performs the insert (no probe-then-mutate race) and returns SubscriptionRegistryError::LineLimitReached when a new line would exceed the cap. SecurityKey fails closed on AssetClass::Option (SecurityKeyError::OptionContractIdentityRequired -> SubscriptionRegistryError::OptionContractUnsupported): a real option contract needs underlying + expiration + strike + right, which is deferred to SRS-DATA-004 / SRS-EXE-004, so the manager rejects option subscriptions rather than conflating distinct contracts on one underlying. SubscriptionChangeEvent carries asset_class so the line-usage stream stays unambiguous. The check PASS line is 'SRS-MD-001 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps passes:false because the AC's runtime halves (real IB feed, async fan-out transport, the <=100 ms latency NFR) are deferred (see deferred[]).

deferred.what: The real IB upstream binding (reqMktData / cancelMktData driven by the registry's Opened / Closed transitions), live tick ingestion, the async fan-out transport that delivers MarketDataTicks to subscriber containers, and thread-safe concurrency. A precise unsupported/invalid-identity error requires either widening SubscriptionLineCounter::try_acquire to a typed result or adding a pre-probe validation stage to MarketDataSubscriptionManager::request_subscription (both MD-002 gate surface) — and lands naturally with the option-support features (which make options representable) plus the operator-interface runtime (which surfaces alerts). backtest_contract.description: SRS-BT-001 (backtest Python strategies against stored data and user-uploaded Parquet data over configurable date ranges). This SDK-surface ships a GENUINELY RUNNABLE deterministic backtest engine in crates/atp-simulation (module `backtest`): BacktestEngine::run takes a BacktestRequest (a configurable inclusive DateRange, a BacktestDataSource = SystemData | UploadedData, and a starting cash balance), a BarSource, and a BacktestStrategy; it restricts replay to the configurable window, replays bars in deterministic timestamp order, drives the strategy bar-by-bar, applies deterministic fills, and produces a real trade log (Vec<Fill>) + equity curve (Vec<EquityPoint>) + final equity. All money math is in integer minor units (i64) with i128 intermediates and checked_add / checked_sub / try_from arithmetic (the free fn checked_notional) — never f64 — so it is exact and overflow-safe (BacktestError::Overflow). The engine fails closed on an empty symbol (EmptySymbol), an inverted window (InvalidDateRange), and a window with no bars (EmptyData). Because the BarSource (uploaded Parquet / system catalog) is a trust boundary, the engine validates provenance — BarSource::source() must equal request.data_source or the run fails closed (DataSourceMismatch), so a result can never misreport which dataset it ran against — and also rejects a source that returns a bar for a different symbol (UnexpectedSymbol — never silently trade foreign prices), duplicate bars for one symbol+timestamp (DuplicateBar — a duplicate market record would double-fill one instant and make replay order-dependent), and any replayed bar with a non-positive close price (NonPositivePrice — a negative close would otherwise flip a buy's checked_sub into a cash-fabricating addition) before any fill is applied. The strategy port BacktestStrategy::on_bar is fallible (returns Result<i64, BacktestError>): because the deferred Python host can raise / time out / fail to marshal, a strategy failure surfaces as StrategyFailed and the engine aborts before applying any fill, never silently treating a failure as a 0 delta. The engine also fails closed (TooManyBars) when the IN-WINDOW replay set exceeds MAX_BACKTEST_BARS — counted AFTER date-range filtering (bars.retain), so the cap is consistent with source-superset tolerance (a large out-of-window response never fails a valid narrow backtest) and bounds the sort + equity-curve allocation; BacktestEngine::with_max_bars sets a custom cap. Bounding the source's own materialization of the raw response needs the full streaming/bounded-cursor BarSource, deferred to the real Parquet reader (see deferred[]). The engine is co-located with the Internal Simulation Engine because SRS-BT-003 mandates the SAME transaction-cost model family for paper simulation and backtesting (home_rationale). Identical inputs produce an identical BacktestResult — the deterministic-replay seam SRS-BT-010 owns. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-001 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-001 passes:false because the data + strategy-execution halves and the REST/dashboard launch surface are deferred behind the BarSource / BacktestStrategy ports (see deferred[]). An operator CLI launch surface for the system-data + configurable-date-range halves now exists (the bt001_backtest_cli binary launches this engine over a --start/--end calendar window via the real StoreBarSource, the date binding being the fail-closed launch::parse_window), but it consumes fixtures and a fixture BacktestStrategy, so there is still no live REST/dashboard launch surface, no real Parquet reader, and no Rust<->Python strategy host yet.
backtest_contract.home_rationale: The SRS module table lists Backtesting and Optimization as a distinct Rust runtime, but SRS-BT-003 mandates the same transaction-cost model family for the internal simulation engine and for backtesting. Co-locating the backtest engine with the InternalSimulationEngine in atp-simulation keeps that shared fill/cost model in one crate; this is the operator-directed home for the slice.

deferred.what: The user-uploaded Parquet BarSource (the real arrow/parquet-backed reader for user-uploaded Parquet files -- a new external Rust dependency that needs SyRS scope approval; the workspace currently has zero third-party crates) and the concrete BacktestStrategy that marshals on_bar calls to a Python strategy running under the orchestrator (the Rust<->Python execution boundary, e.g. PyO3 or a subprocess strategy host, also SyRS-gated). The SYSTEM-CATALOG BarSource is now LANDED by store_bar_source::StoreBarSource (SRS-DATA-007), which reads the unified MarketDataStore (query_unified raw / gated query_split_adjusted) with no provider named -- so BacktestDataSource::SystemData is served by shipped product code. The engine still models the deterministic replay + integer-money fill loop only; the user-uploaded Parquet reader + the real Python strategy host flip SRS-BT-001 passes:true.

The live REST handler for POST /api/v1/backtests (declared today as route metadata in python/atp_api/routes.py, which explicitly does not start an HTTP server) and the dashboard backtest controls with start/end date pickers. The operator CLI half of this launch surface is now LANDED: the bt001_backtest_cli binary (crates/atp-simulation/src/bin/bt001_backtest_cli.rs) launches the runnable engine over an operator-supplied --start/--end calendar window via the real store_bar_source::StoreBarSource system-data path, binding YYYY-MM-DD dates to the engine's DateRange through launch::parse_window (a fail-closed, integer-only, dependency-free calendar binding) and failing closed on a deferred uploaded-Parquet source, a non-positive starting cash, an unseeded symbol, and any unknown/malformed/inverted input. The acceptance criterion 'start and end dates are selectable through API AND dashboard' still needs the REST + dashboard operator-interface runtime, which remains deferred (and reuses the same launch::parse_window binding); the engine already accepts a configurable DateRange and echoes it on BacktestResult.range.

BarSource::bars takes a max_bars cap (the engine passes self.max_bars) and its contract requires a source to fail closed with TooManyBars before materializing an oversized Vec; the engine re-checks the in-window count as defense in depth. The FULLY streaming/bounded sorted cursor that never materializes a Vec at all (chunked record-batch iteration) is still deferred to the real arrow/parquet-backed reader, because that shape depends on the library's reader API; the bounded max_bars parameter is the forward-compatible seam, and the Vec port has no concrete implementors yet, so it is not a baked-in external contract. Likewise the fallible strategy port surfaces failures as the single StrategyFailed { reason } today; the typed StrategyTimedOut / StrategyPanicked / marshaling-error taxonomy lands with the concrete Rust<->Python strategy host (same reason-string-now / typed-later pattern as the kill_switch_timeout_contract probe error). Raised by /codex:adversarial-review round-2: the symbol/duplicate/non-positive-price/strategy-failure guards were added in this slice; the streaming-cursor redesign is deferred per the scope decision.

SRS-BT-002 AND SRS-BT-003 are now IMPLEMENTED on this engine: the configurable transaction-cost model family (commission / slippage / spread-impact, SyRS-default matching) lives in the `cost` module and BacktestEngine::run applies it per fill via the per-run BacktestRequest.cost_config — see the backtest_cost_contract block; the internal simulation engine shares the SAME family for paper fills via the `sim` module (SRS-BT-003 passes:true — see sim_cost_contract). BT-001's own engine tests still run frictionless (CostConfig::zero). Still deferred for BT-001 itself: the REST/dashboard cost-override surface (SRS-API-001/SRS-UI) and the real data + Python strategy runtime (SRS-BT-001-runtime).

The full performance metric set (Sharpe, Sortino, alpha, beta, maximum drawdown, annualized return/volatility, win rate). The engine emits the raw trade log + equity curve those metrics are computed from; the metric computations are SRS-BT-004's concern.

Persisted, queryable backtest results (parameters, metrics, trade log, equity curve, benchmark comparison, strategy code version, timestamp). BacktestResult is the in-memory shape; the durable store + query API are SRS-BT-009.

The end-to-end deterministic-backtest guarantee under the real Python strategy host (two identical runs produce identical trade logs, equity curves, and metrics). This slice demonstrates determinism for the Rust engine (stable sort_by_key replay + integer money math); the guarantee across the full Python execution path is SRS-BT-010.

backtest_cost_contract.description: SRS-BT-002 (apply configurable commission, slippage, and spread-impact models to backtests). The configurable transaction-cost model FAMILY lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `cost`) and is APPLIED by the runnable backtest engine (module `backtest`). CostConfig bundles three configurable models: CommissionModel (default IbTiered = the published Interactive Brokers tiered schedule — IB_TIERED_RATE_CENTIMINOR_PER_SHARE per share, floored at IB_TIERED_MIN_PER_ORDER_MINOR per order, capped at IB_TIERED_MAX_PCT_BPS of trade value, SYS-15a), SlippageModel (default NotionalBps at DEFAULT_SLIPPAGE_BPS = 0.05% of trade notional, SYS-15b), and SpreadImpactModel (default ObservedOrFallbackBps = half the observed bid-ask spread per share when the bar carries one, else DEFAULT_SPREAD_FALLBACK_BPS = 0.10% of notional, SYS-15c). CostConfig::default() is the SyRS baseline encoded as named constants, so the default is PROVABLY the published value; an operator overrides any model for an individual run (SYS-15d) by building a different CostConfig on BacktestRequest.cost_config — no strategy code changes. All cost math is integer minor units (i128 intermediates, deterministic round-half-up division via div_round_half_up, i64::try_from narrowing -> CostError::Overflow; never f64); every cost is non-negative and the engine always SUBTRACTS it from cash via checked_sub, so a cost can never fabricate cash. The engine fails closed before any fill on a negative configured parameter (CostConfig::validate -> CostError::NegativeParameter) and on a corrupt negative observed bid-ask spread on ANY bar (BacktestError::NegativeSpread, mirroring the BT-001 non-positive-price guard). cost.rs is the SAME transaction-cost model family the internal simulation engine shares for paper fills (SRS-BT-003, now closed/passes:true — see sim_cost_contract). The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-002 SDK-SURFACE PASS'. The operator override SURFACE the acceptance names is now REALIZED for the CLI: the bt002_cost_cli operator binary (`defaults` + `run`, see cost_cli) builds a per-run CostConfig from --commission/--slippage/--spread flags, sets it on BacktestRequest.cost_config, and runs the SAME fixture strategy, demonstrating BOTH halves of the AC end to end in a fresh process (defaults match SyRS values; per-run override without changing strategy code, SYS-15d); the srs_bt_002_cost_cli integration test pins it. feature_list.json is now passes:true. The REST + dashboard half of that surface (SRS-API-001 / SRS-UI) and the real data + Python strategy runtime (SRS-BT-001-runtime) remain deferred (see deferred[]).

shared_family.note: SRS-BT-003 shares the cost::CostConfig family for internal simulation paper fills.

cost_cli.note: The CLI half of the SYS-15d operator override surface: `defaults` prints the SyRS constants and proves CostConfig::default() == CostConfig::syrs_defaults() (defaults match SyRS values); `run` builds a per-run CostConfig from the --commission/--slippage/--spread flags, sets it on BacktestRequest.cost_config, and runs the SAME fixture strategy, so the realized per-fill costs change while the strategy does not (override without changing strategy code). A negative override parameter is rejected by CostConfig::validate inside the engine before any fill (no cash fabricated). The srs_bt_002_cost_cli integration test drives the binary in fresh processes. The REST + dashboard half (SRS-API-001 / SRS-UI) and the real data + Python strategy host (SRS-BT-001-runtime) remain deferred.

deferred.what: The CLI half of the SYS-15d operator override surface is now REALIZED (the bt002_cost_cli binary builds a per-run CostConfig from flags and applies it without strategy changes; see cost_cli). The remaining deferred half is the REST endpoint + dashboard form that construct the override from an API payload / web control for an individual backtest run; that surface (SRS-API-001 / SRS-UI) is owned by those features, not SRS-BT-002.

CLOSED (passes:true): the internal simulation engine consumes this exact cost::CostConfig family for paper fills via the `sim` module -- PaperSimulationEngine::simulate_fill calls the IDENTICAL CostConfig::cost_breakdown entry point the backtest engine calls, defaults to the same SyRS baseline (SYS-15e), and the equality is proven fill-for-fill and made operator-demonstrable by bt003_shared_cost_cli (`compare` -> cost-family-match:true). See `sim_cost_contract`. SRS-BT-003's acceptance criterion is fully met; the items in sim_cost_contract.deferred[] (SYS-83 limit/stop/fill-probability/volume-cap fill models, live-market-data fills, the full SYS-84 ledger, paper-state persistence, and the REST/dashboard cost-override surface) are ADJACENT requirements (SRS-SIM-002/003/004, SRS-API-001/SRS-UI, SRS-BT-001-runtime), each owned and tracked on its own feature, NOT SRS-BT-003 deferrals.

IbTiered models the published base (<=300k shares/month) per-share rate, the per-order minimum, and the 1%-of-trade-value cap. The cumulative monthly-volume tier ladder (cheaper per-share rates at higher monthly volume) needs cross-run state and is deferred; the per-run rate/floor/cap is the SyRS default baseline.

Same deferral as SRS-BT-001: the real Parquet / system-catalog BarSource and the Rust<->Python strategy host. The cost models are applied by the runnable engine over the in-memory port fixtures today; a true end-to-end pass also needs that runtime.

sim_cost_contract.description: SRS-BT-003 (use the same transaction-cost model family for internal simulation and backtesting unless configured otherwise; SyRS SYS-15e / SYS-83d; StRS SN-1.03 / SN-1.29). The internal simulation engine applies the SAME transaction-cost model FAMILY (crates/atp-simulation module `cost`, SRS-BT-002) the runnable backtest engine applies, via the new module `sim` paper-fill path. PaperSimulationEngine holds a `cost_config: CostConfig` that derives Default = CostConfig::default(), the IDENTICAL SyRS baseline the backtest engine defaults to (SYS-15e 'same default ... unless explicitly configured otherwise'); an operator overrides it per strategy via with_cost_config without touching strategy code. simulate_fill computes the per-fill CostBreakdown by calling self.cost_config.cost_breakdown(quantity, price_minor, observed_spread_minor) -- the IDENTICAL entry point BacktestEngine::run calls -- and folds the total into a signed cash_delta_minor that ALWAYS SUBTRACTS the cost (a buy pays notional plus cost; a sell receives proceeds less cost), so a cost can never fabricate cash. All money math is integer minor units (i128::from intermediates, i64::try_from -> SimError::Overflow, checked_*; never f64). The fill fails closed before computing any cost on an empty symbol (SimError::EmptySymbol), a non-positive price (SimError::NonPositivePrice), a negative observed spread (SimError::NegativeSpread), and a misconfigured negative cost parameter (SimError::Cost(CostError::NegativeParameter)). The minimal PaperLedger accumulates cash, position, and commission_paid (the full SYS-84 virtual ledger is SRS-SIM-003). The Rust integration test srs_bt_003_shared_cost_family asserts the paper fill's commission / slippage / spread-impact EQUAL the backtest fill's for identical config and inputs (the SRS-BT-003 acceptance criterion), and the operator binary bt003_shared_cost_cli (see `shared_cost_cli`) makes that equality operator-demonstrable: `compare` runs the SAME fixture strategy over the SAME bars through BOTH engines under one CostConfig and prints cost-family-match:true, driven in fresh processes by the L5 srs_bt_003_cost_cli. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-003 SDK-SURFACE PASS'. feature_list.json now marks SRS-BT-003 passes:true: the acceptance criterion -- 'a paper strategy and backtest using identical cost configuration compute fills and commissions from the same model family' -- is single-context (both engines are built) and fully demonstrated fill-for-fill by the L5 + L7 + the operator CLI. The items in deferred[] are ADJACENT simulation features (the richer SYS-83 fill models, the full SYS-84 ledger, paper-state persistence, the REST/dashboard override surface, the Python strategy host) -- they are each their OWN requirement and are NOT part of SRS-BT-003's narrow shared-cost-family acceptance criterion, so they do not gate this flip.

shared_family.note: lib.rs re-exports both `pub mod cost;` and `pub mod sim;`; the internal simulation engine and the backtest engine apply the SAME cost::CostConfig family through the identical CostConfig::cost_breakdown entry point (SRS-BT-003 / SYS-15e).

shared_cost_cli.note: The operator-demonstrable surface of the SRS-BT-003 acceptance criterion: `defaults` proves PaperSimulationEngine::default().cost_config() == CostConfig::default() == CostConfig::syrs_defaults() (SYS-15e same family); `compare` runs the SAME fixture strategy over the SAME bars through BOTH the real BacktestEngine and a PaperSimulationEngine replay under one CostConfig and prints each engine's per-fill commission/slippage/spread-impact, asserting they are EQUAL (cost-family-match:true). Every --inject fault (nonpositive-price/negative-spread/negative-commission) corrupts a SHARED input and is rejected by BOTH engines before any fill (both engines failed closed; non-zero exit; no comparison line). Driven in fresh processes by the L5 srs_bt_003_cost_cli.
