This slice computes the shared cost decomposition over explicit (quantity, price, spread) fill inputs; the order-type fill logic, volume constraints, and live-data fill source are SRS-SIM-002's half.

The full SYS-84 virtual position ledger -- per-symbol average cost, realized and unrealized P&L marked to live data, independent per strategy. PaperLedger here is a minimal cash + position + commission-paid accumulator that proves the shared family's commissions actually accumulate; the full ledger is SRS-SIM-003.
Persisting and restoring the paper ledger, pending simulated orders, and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89).

The CLI half is REALIZED by bt003_shared_cost_cli: `compare --commission/--slippage/--spread` constructs one per-run CostConfig and applies it to BOTH engines, the operator override-selection workflow the acceptance names. The REST/dashboard controls that let an operator select or override the per-strategy cost model from the web surface remain the deferred half (SRS-API-001 / SRS-UI); this is a SEPARATE requirement and not part of SRS-BT-003's shared-cost-family acceptance criterion.

The Python strategy host that drives paper fills end to end is its own deferred requirement (SRS-BT-001-runtime / SRS-SDK). It is NOT part of SRS-BT-003's acceptance criterion: like every closed backtest-thread feature (SRS-BT-002/006/009/010), the shared-cost-family criterion is demonstrated over a fixture strategy run through the real engines (the bt003_shared_cost_cli `compare` workflow + the L5/L7 tests), which is the 'Test' verification method SRS-BT-003 specifies. The richer real-data + Python-runtime path remains deferred for the features that genuinely need it.

sim_order_contract.description: SRS-SIM-001 (simulate paper strategy orders locally without routing to any brokerage; SyRS SYS-82 local paper order execution, SYS-3 order types, SYS-4 multi-leg composite; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-1.08 / SN-1.24). The internal simulation engine's paper order-intake path lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `paper_order`) and adds an `accept_order` method to PaperSimulationEngine that accepts a PaperOrderRequest (a single OrderLeg or a MultiLeg composite), validates every leg, and returns an OrderRouting. The CRITICAL safety design: OrderRouting has exactly ONE variant, InternalSimulation, so there is structurally no Broker/Ib variant to construct and a paper order can never reach a brokerage -- 'creates no IB API order calls' (SRS-SIM-001) is a COMPILE-TIME guarantee, not a runtime check. The atp-simulation crate also has no dependency on atp-execution or atp-adapters (the live/broker path), reinforcing the no-broker-route invariant at the crate boundary. accept_order processes every OrderType (Market, Limit, Stop, StopLimit) and AssetClass (Equity, Option); a MultiLeg request routes as one composite transaction (composite: true, SYS-4) so its legs fill atomically, and because SYS-4 / SRS-EXE-004 scope multi-leg to OPTIONS composites a MultiLeg must carry two or more legs that are all options. Intake fails closed before routing on an empty symbol (OrderError::EmptySymbol), a non-positive quantity (NonPositiveQuantity), a non-positive limit/stop price (NonPositiveLimitPrice / NonPositiveStopPrice), an empty multi-leg request (EmptyMultiLeg), a single-leg composite (SingleLegComposite), and a non-option composite leg (NonOptionCompositeLeg); the per-leg guards live in the private validate_leg helper and the composite-shape guards in accept_order. Every price an order carries is an integer minor unit with the _minor suffix (limit_price_minor, stop_price_minor); intake performs no fill arithmetic and the module contains no f64. The Rust integration test srs_sim_001_paper_order_intake asserts every order type / asset class / single + multi-leg request routes to InternalSimulation and that the routing enum exposes no broker variant, and the operator binary sim001_paper_order_cli (the `order_cli` sub-block) makes the acceptance criterion operator-demonstrable: `types` routes every SYS-3 order type internally (all-order-types-routed:true), `assets` routes both asset classes internally (both-asset-classes-routed:true), `multileg` routes a SYS-4 spread as one composite (composite-routed:true), and `no-broker` sweeps every order shape and proves none reaches a brokerage (no-ib-order-calls:true). The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-001 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; with the engine, the L5 srs_sim_001_paper_order_intake, and that operator surface all built, feature_list.json marks SRS-SIM-001 passes:true. The items under `deferred` are genuinely ADJACENT features -- each its own requirement and NOT contexts inside SRS-SIM-001's acceptance criterion: the SYS-83 fill TRIGGERING (price-cross fills, fill probability, bar-volume cap) + live-market-data fills are SRS-SIM-002 (built, passes:true), the full SYS-84 virtual ledger is SRS-SIM-003 (built, passes:true), paper-state persistence (SYS-89) is SRS-SIM-004, the orchestrator routing of all non-live strategies into this engine is SRS-EXE-002, and the Python strategy runtime that submits these orders end to end is the shared SRS-SDK deferral.

deferred.feature: SRS-SIM-002 (fill models + live-data fills) -- built, passes:true
deferred.what: The SYS-83 fill TRIGGERING for market/limit/stop/stop-limit orders (fill at ask/bid for market, fill on price cross for limit, trigger on stop cross), the configurable fill model, the bar-volume fill cap, and fills driven by live bid/ask/last from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70). This slice accepts, validates, and routes the orders; the logic that turns a routed order into a fill is SRS-SIM-002's half -- a SEPARATE requirement (now built and passes:true), NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-001's acceptance criterion.

Applying accepted/filled orders to the full SYS-84 virtual position ledger (per-symbol average cost, realized and unrealized P&L), independent per strategy. SRS-SIM-001 stops at routing; the ledger application is SRS-SIM-003 -- a SEPARATE requirement (now built and passes:true), NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-001's acceptance criterion.
Persisting and restoring pending simulated orders and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89). A SEPARATE requirement, NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-001's acceptance criterion.

The Strategy Orchestrator wiring that routes ALL non-live strategy orders into this engine. accept_order is the per-engine intake; the system-level guarantee that every non-live strategy lands here (and only the single live strategy reaches IB) is SRS-EXE-002 / SRS-EXE-001 -- a SEPARATE requirement, NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-001's acceptance criterion.

Same adjacency as the SRS-BT/SRS-SIM siblings: the Python strategy host that actually submits these orders end to end. Intake is exercised over in-memory request fixtures and the sim001_paper_order_cli operator surface; the AC's named contexts (every order type and asset class, the multi-leg composite, and the no-IB-route guarantee) are all built and operator-demonstrable, so feature_list.json marks SRS-SIM-001 passes:true. The Python runtime is a shared adjacent deferral, NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-001's acceptance criterion.

sim_fill_contract.description: SRS-SIM-002 (simulate fills using live market data and configurable fill models; SyRS SYS-83 fill simulation, SYS-87 realism constraints; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-1.03). The internal simulation engine's fill-model / triggering path lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `fill_model`) and adds an `evaluate_fill` method to PaperSimulationEngine that turns a routed OrderType (from the SRS-SIM-001 intake path) plus a MarketSnapshot (bid, ask, last, volume from the SYS-70 subscription manager) into a FillDecision. The SYS-83 fill rules: a Market order fills at the ask (buy) or bid (sell); a Limit order fills only when the market crosses the limit under the configurable per-strategy LimitFillModel and takes the limit price as its conservative no-improvement reference. Two behavior-changing deterministic limit models ship: ImmediateOnCross (the SYS-83b default) fills a touch at the limit (buy when ask <= limit, sell when bid >= limit), while RequireThroughCross fills only on a strict cross (buy when ask < limit, sell when bid > limit), so the two configs produce different decisions on the same touch snapshot. a Stop order triggers when the last trade crosses the stop (buy when last >= stop, sell when last <= stop) and then fills as a market order; a StopLimit order triggers at the stop then rests as a limit. The SYS-87b volume constraint is enforced both per-order and in aggregate: evaluate_fill caps a single order at the bar volume (fill_quantity = min(requested, bar_volume)), while evaluate_fill_against_budget caps each fill at a per-bar BarVolumeBudget's REMAINING volume and CONSUMES it, so the SUM of fills against one bar (the SYS-87b 'bar period') cannot exceed its observed volume even across orders; evaluate_fill is exactly evaluate_fill_against_budget with a fresh per-call budget. The budget is BOUND to its bar: evaluate_fill_against_budget fails closed with FillModelError::BudgetSnapshotMismatch when the budget's observed_bar_volume does not equal the snapshot's bar_volume, so a stale or oversized budget cannot be used against a thinner bar to fill past its observed volume. An exhausted bar yields NoFillReason::ZeroVolume, the trigger/cross test runs BEFORE the cap so an order that never crosses reports LimitNotCrossed / StopNotTriggered and consumes no budget, and the bar-replay loop that sequences orders within a bar is a deferred simulation-engine-runtime concern (the SRS-SIM-003 virtual ledger that records the resulting fills is built; sequencing multiple orders within one bar is not the ledger's responsibility). evaluate_fill is &self with no clock or randomness, so it is deterministic. It fails closed BEFORE any fill decision on a non-positive requested quantity (FillModelError::NonPositiveQuantity), a non-positive limit/stop price on the raw order type (NonPositiveLimitPrice / NonPositiveStopPrice via validate_order_type, so a negative limit can never cross a valid snapshot and return a fill at that negative price), a non-positive quote (NonPositiveQuote), a crossed book (CrossedBook), and a negative bar volume (NegativeVolume); the order-price guards live in validate_order_type and the snapshot guards in validate_snapshot. A FillDecision::Filled feeds simulate_fill (SRS-BT-003), so a triggered fill flows through the SAME transaction-cost family the backtest engine applies and stays entirely inside the internal simulation engine -- it creates no IB API order call, reusing the SRS-SIM-001 no-broker-route crate-boundary guarantee (atp-simulation depends on neither atp-execution nor atp-adapters). Every price is an integer minor unit with the _minor suffix; the module contains no f64. The Rust integration test srs_sim_002_fill_models asserts every order type resolves a deterministic decision, limit/stop orders hold until the market crosses, the two limit fill models produce different decisions on a touch, the per-order volume cap is enforced, the aggregate of fills against one BarVolumeBudget cannot exceed the bar volume, a budget that does not match the snapshot's bar volume fails closed (cannot overfill a thinner bar), a filled decision flows through the cost family, corrupt market data fails closed, and no malformed (negative/zero) limit/stop price can produce a fill. The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-002 SDK-SURFACE PASS'. The operator-demonstrable surface is the sim002_fill_cli binary (subcommands defaults / rules / config / volume over the real evaluate_fill engine: it proves the SYS-83 reference price for each of the four order types, that the two per-strategy LimitFillModels diverge on the same touch snapshot, and that the SYS-87b cap holds per-order and in aggregate, with every injected fault failing closed BEFORE any fill decision and no proof line emitted under a fault), driven in fresh processes by the L5 srs_sim_002_fill_cli. With that surface realized, feature_list.json marks SRS-SIM-002 passes:true: every context named in the acceptance criterion -- the market/limit/stop/stop-limit fills, the SYS-83 defaults, the per-strategy configuration, and the SYS-87b volume constraint -- is built and operator-demonstrable over the Rust core (there is no Python<->Rust strategy host, so the operator workflow is demonstrated there, the same precedent as the SRS-BT-002 / BT-003 / BT-009 / BT-010 / SIM-003 CLIs). The remaining items are ADJACENT features, NOT contexts inside SRS-SIM-002's acceptance criterion, each a separate requirement with its own owner: the SYS-87a market-hours gate (needs the SYS-50 trading calendar), the SYS-87c stale-data threshold (SRS-MD-004 / SYS-39 freshness), the live bid/ask/last feed from the SYS-70 subscription manager (the MarketSnapshot models that live-market-data shape; the feed wiring is SYS-70's half), the STOCHASTIC fill-probability model (SYS-83b -- the two deterministic LimitFillModel variants already satisfy the configurable-fill-model requirement), applying fills to the SYS-84 virtual ledger (SRS-SIM-003, itself built and passes:true), paper-state persistence (SRS-SIM-004 / SYS-89), the orchestrator routing of all non-live strategies (SRS-EXE-002), and the Python strategy runtime.

deferred.what: Gating simulated fills to market hours per the trading calendar (SYS-87a). evaluate_fill models the price/volume realism of a fill; the market-hours gate needs the SYS-50 trading-calendar service, which is unbuilt, so it is deferred to SRS-MD / the calendar feature.

Rejecting order submissions when market data is stale per the configurable staleness threshold (SYS-87c / SYS-39a). This slice validates the SHAPE of the snapshot (positive quotes, an uncrossed book, non-negative volume) but does not yet consult a freshness clock; the staleness-threshold check belongs on the SYS-70 fan-out / freshness_contract seam and is deferred.

Sourcing the MarketSnapshot's live bid/ask/last/volume from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70) fan-out. The snapshot is an in-memory fixture today; the live feed wiring is the SYS-70 / SRS-MD owner's half.

The STOCHASTIC fill-probability variant of LimitFillModel (SYS-83b 'subject to configurable fill probability model'). Two DETERMINISTIC, behavior-changing limit models already ship (ImmediateOnCross fills a touch, RequireThroughCross requires a strict cross), so the SRS-SIM-002 configurable-fill-model acceptance path is met; a probabilistic model that fills a crossed limit with probability p < 1 needs a seeded RNG and is deferred to keep evaluate_fill deterministic. The LimitFillModel enum is the structural seam it will extend.

This is a separate, already-closed feature, NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-002's acceptance criterion.
Persisting and restoring pending simulated orders and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89).

Same deferral as the SRS-BT / SRS-SIM siblings: the Python strategy host that submits the orders these fills satisfy. The fill model is exercised over in-memory snapshot fixtures and the operator workflow is demonstrated over the Rust core (sim002_fill_cli); the Python<->Rust strategy host is a separate adjacent requirement, NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-002's acceptance criterion.

virtual_ledger_contract.description: SRS-SIM-003 (maintain an independent virtual position ledger for each paper strategy; SyRS SYS-84; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-1.07). The internal simulation engine's per-strategy virtual position ledger lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `virtual_ledger`) and consumes a priced sim::PaperFill (the SRS-BT-003 cost path) without any new dependency. Three nested layers, each isolated from the next: VirtualPosition is one symbol's signed quantity (i64), signed cost_basis_minor (i128, the source of truth for average cost: positive for a long = cash laid out, negative for a short = proceeds received), accumulated realized_pnl_minor (i128, GROSS of every transaction cost), and the FULL transaction-cost decomposition the cost family charges -- commission_paid_minor, slippage_paid_minor, and spread_impact_paid_minor (each i128), summed by transaction_cost_paid_minor. Tracking every cost component (not just commission) keeps the ledger faithful to the simulator: a closed position's realized_pnl_minor minus transaction_cost_paid_minor reconciles EXACTLY with the sum of the fills' cash_delta_minor (the simulator's actual cash impact), so no charged cost (commission, slippage, or spread) silently disappears from the ledger. StrategyLedger holds one paper strategy's VirtualPosition values keyed by symbol (positions: HashMap<String, VirtualPosition>); the symbol is canonicalized via canonical_symbol (symbol.trim().to_uppercase(), the same policy as atp_types::SecurityKey) before every key and lookup, so AAPL / aapl / ' AAPL ' resolve to ONE position instead of splitting one security's quantity and P&L across aliases. The ledger's instrument identity is the canonical symbol STRING, which uniquely identifies the instrument under the system's symbol conventions (equities by ticker, options by the full OCC contract string such as 'AAPL  240119C00190000', so an option and its underlying do NOT share a key). A SecurityKey is not used directly because the simulated fill (sim::PaperFill) carries a bare symbol with NO asset class and SecurityKey rejects options that SRS-SIM-001 intake accepts; formal (symbol + asset_class + full option-contract) keying needs the upstream PaperFill / simulate_fill cost-path contract (SRS-BT-003 / SRS-SIM-001) to carry that identity, which is the Data Layer's normalization responsibility and is DEFERRED (see deferred[]). VirtualLedgerBook holds every paper strategy's ledger keyed by StrategyId (ledgers: HashMap<StrategyId, StrategyLedger>), and apply_fill routes a fill to exactly one strategy's ledger via self.ledgers.get_mut(strategy) (existing) or self.ledgers.insert(strategy.clone(), ledger) on first touch, so one strategy's fills can never read or mutate another's quantities or P&L, and the book holds NO IB / broker account position at all (it imports no broker, adapter, or live-position type), so a virtual position is structurally independent of the IB account (SYS-84). The book and the per-strategy ledger both insert a new entry ONLY after the fill applies successfully (the fresh value is built and ledger.apply_fill(fill)? / position.apply_fill(fill)? propagates the error BEFORE the insert), so a rejected first fill never leaves a phantom strategy or phantom symbol behind to pollute later metrics, persistence, or orchestrator accounting. Average-cost accounting on apply_fill: opening or adding in the same direction grows cost_basis by q*px and quantity by q with no realized P&L; reducing or closing (q opposes the held quantity, |q| <= |held|) releases the PROPORTIONAL slice of the basis cost_removed = cost_basis * |q| / |held| (cost_basis.checked_mul(fill_abs) / held_abs), recognises realized += -(q*px) - cost_removed, drops cost_removed from the basis, and adds q to the quantity, so closing the whole position releases EXACTLY the whole basis and leaves cost_basis at 0; flipping through zero (|q| > |held|) fully closes the existing position (releasing all its basis and recognising its realized P&L) then opens the remainder q_open = q + held at px as a fresh position. Realized P&L is kept GROSS of commission and commission_paid_minor accumulates SEPARATELY (SYS-84 lists them as distinct fields). average_cost_minor returns cost_basis / quantity (truncated, None when flat) -- a DERIVED view of the exact signed cost basis. The rounding policy is chosen for EXACT BASIS CONSERVATION: cost_removed truncates toward zero and the remainder stays in the residual basis, so across any fill sequence realized P&L plus the remaining basis equals the exact cash from prices and a full close always lands at cost_basis == 0 (no value created or destroyed). The deliberate trade-off is that the truncated per-unit average_cost_minor can shift by up to one minor unit on a partial close even with no acquisition; the basis itself is always exact, and the conservation invariant is pinned by a property-style unit test over many generated fill sequences (including an indivisible basis-32-over-3 case). unrealized_pnl_minor marks the open position to market against the live MarketSnapshot's last trade price (mark * quantity - cost_basis, exact and correct for both longs and shorts, 0 when flat). StrategyLedger and VirtualLedgerBook expose a SYMBOL-KEYED unrealized_pnl_minor(symbol, snapshot) surface that selects the position BY the named symbol, so a quote is never applied to a different instrument's position by mistake; rejecting a wrong-symbol quote by its CONTENT needs the SRS-SIM-002 MarketSnapshot to carry instrument identity (it is an identity-free fixture today), which is deferred with the SYS-70 live feed. It fails closed BEFORE any mutation on a non-positive fill price (LedgerError::NonPositiveFillPrice), a zero-quantity fill (LedgerError::ZeroQuantityFill), a negative transaction-cost component (LedgerError::NegativeCost{component} -- PaperFill fields are public, so the ledger validates commission, slippage, and spread_impact rather than trusting them, since a negative value would decrease its accumulator and could fabricate cash on reconciliation), a cash_delta_minor that disagrees with -(notional) - total_cost (LedgerError::InconsistentCashDelta -- the ledger does not accumulate cash from the public cash_delta field but enforces the simulate_fill invariant on it before mutating, so a tampered/inconsistent fill cannot silently break the reconciliation guarantee), an empty symbol (LedgerError::EmptySymbol, at the StrategyLedger seam), a non-positive mark (LedgerError::NonPositiveMark), and money-math overflow (LedgerError::Overflow). VirtualPosition::apply_fill is ATOMIC: it computes every prospective value (quantity, cost basis, realized P&L, commission) into locals with checked arithmetic and commits them to the position only after EVERY checked op succeeds, so an overflow in the basis/quantity accounting can never leave a moved commission behind that a retry would double-count. apply_fill is &mut self with no clock or randomness, so the ledger is deterministic. Every quantity and money figure is an integer minor unit; intermediates are i128 with checked_* accumulation; the module contains no f64. The Rust integration test srs_sim_003_virtual_ledger drives the ledger end to end from real simulate_fill output and asserts a long round trip realizes price-based P&L while commission accumulates separately, average cost blends across adds, mark-to-market values longs and shorts, a flip through zero realizes only the closed portion and reopens at the fill price, two strategies holding the same symbol stay independent (mutating one leaves the other untouched), corrupt input fails closed, aliased symbols (AAPL / aapl / ' AAPL ') share ONE position, and a rejected first fill for a brand-new strategy leaves no phantom strategy behind (strategy_count stays 0). The unit tests additionally prove a negative cost component (commission / slippage / spread) fails closed, an inconsistent cash_delta_minor fails closed, every cost component accumulates, a rejected fill rolls back atomically including the commission (a rejected fill leaves the position byte-for-byte unchanged), basis conservation holds over many generated fill sequences (full close to cost_basis == 0 with exact realized P&L, even for an indivisible basis), the ledger reconciles with the simulator -- a round trip's realized_pnl_minor minus transaction_cost_paid_minor equals the sum of the fills' cash_delta_minor, and a same-price round trip nets a loss exactly equal to the costs charged (no cost silently disappears), and the symbol-keyed marking surface selects the position named by the symbol. The operator binary sim003_ledger_cli makes the acceptance criterion operator-demonstrable: its `isolate` subcommand opens the SAME symbol under two paper strategies (alpha, beta), prints all five SYS-84 quantities per strategy, and proves they are isolated per strategy (ledger-isolation:true -- mutating one leaves the other byte-for-byte unchanged while the mutated one DID change) and account-independent:true (two strategies holding one symbol resolve to DIFFERENT positions, which a single shared IB account position could never produce), while failing closed on any injected fault (no isolation line is ever printed over a corrupt fill); `defaults` shows a fresh book holds zero strategies and no IB account position. The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-003 SDK-SURFACE PASS'. Every named context in the SRS-SIM-003 acceptance criterion (quantity, average cost, unrealized P&L, realized P&L, and commission paid isolated per paper strategy and independent of IB account positions) is built and demonstrated over the Rust core, so feature_list.json marks SRS-SIM-003 passes:true. The following are genuinely ADJACENT features -- separate requirements that touch the paper-trading stack but are NOT contexts inside SRS-SIM-003's acceptance criterion: the live bid/ask/last feed that drives the mark comes from the SYS-70 subscription manager (the MarketSnapshot is an in-memory fixture today), corporate-action adjustment of virtual positions (SYS-88) is SRS-DATA-021, ledger persistence and restore (SYS-89) is SRS-SIM-004, the accumulated paper performance metrics (SYS-85) are their own feature, the orchestrator routing of all non-live strategies into this engine is SRS-EXE-002, and the Python strategy runtime is still deferred.

deferred.feature: Asset-class / option-contract instrument identity in the position key
deferred.what: The SyRS Position (Virtual) model lists asset class alongside symbol, but the ledger keys positions by the canonical symbol STRING only, because the upstream sim::PaperFill (the SRS-BT-003 cost-path output) carries a bare symbol with NO asset class. Under the system's symbol conventions distinct instruments already get distinct keys (equities by ticker, options by the full OCC contract string), so no actual merge occurs today; formal (symbol + asset_class + full option-contract) keying needs PaperFill / simulate_fill to carry that identity, which is a cross-feature change to the SRS-BT-003 / SRS-SIM-001 contract and is deferred to that owner rather than threaded through this ledger slice.

The mark-to-market surface selects the position BY symbol (StrategyLedger / VirtualLedgerBook unrealized_pnl_minor(symbol, snapshot)), so a quote is never applied to a different instrument's position. But the SRS-SIM-002 MarketSnapshot carries no instrument identity (it is an identity-free in-memory fixture pending the SYS-70 live feed), so the ledger cannot reject a snapshot whose CONTENT is actually another instrument's quote. Binding the snapshot to an instrument (so a wrong-symbol quote fails closed) needs MarketSnapshot to carry identity, which is the SRS-SIM-002 / SYS-70 owner's shape and is deferred with the live feed.

apply_fill is a PURE state transition: it applies exactly the fill it is given, so replaying an already-applied fill double-counts quantity, basis, P&L, and costs. Deduplicating duplicate delivery needs (a) a unique execution/fill identifier on sim::PaperFill -- which it does not carry -- a cross-feature change to the SRS-BT-003 cost-path contract, and (b) exactly-once delivery semantics owned by the orchestrator routing layer (SRS-EXE-002), which routes non-live strategy fills into this engine and is itself deferred. SYS-84 / SRS-SIM-003 specify the ledger's tracked fields, not delivery semantics, so dedup is deferred to the PaperFill-identity + SRS-EXE-002 owners rather than guessed at here (a content-hash dedup would wrongly drop two legitimately-identical fills).

Sourcing the MarketSnapshot's live last trade price (the mark-to-market reference) from the market data subscription manager (SYS-70) fan-out. The snapshot is an in-memory fixture today; the live feed wiring is the SYS-70 / SRS-MD owner's half, so unrealized P&L is marked against a fixture, not a live feed.

Adjusting virtual position quantities and average cost for splits, dividends, and mergers, and cancelling virtual orders for delisted securities, using the same corporate-action data as live trading and backtesting (SYS-88). This slice keeps the raw fill-driven ledger; the corporate-action adjustment is SRS-DATA-021.
Persisting and restoring the virtual ledger, pending simulated orders, and accumulated metrics across container restarts (SYS-89). The ledger is in-memory here; persistence is SRS-SIM-004.

Computing the accumulated paper performance metrics (returns, drawdown, ratios) over the ledger's realized/unrealized P&L (SYS-85). The ledger exposes the per-position P&L primitives; the metric aggregation is its own feature (the SRS-BT-004 metric family for paper).

The Strategy Orchestrator wiring that routes ALL non-live strategy fills into this ledger. VirtualLedgerBook is the per-engine ledger surface keyed by StrategyId; the system-level guarantee that every non-live strategy's fills land here (and only the single live strategy reaches IB) is SRS-EXE-002 / SRS-EXE-001.

Same adjacent deferral as the SRS-BT / SRS-SIM siblings: the Python strategy host whose orders produce the fills this ledger records end to end. The ledger is exercised over in-memory PaperFill fixtures today; the Python runtime is a SEPARATE requirement and is NOT a context inside SRS-SIM-003's acceptance criterion (which is the per-strategy isolation of the five quantities, demonstrated over the Rust core), so it does not gate the flip -- feature_list.json marks SRS-SIM-003 passes:true.

paper_halt_contract.description: SRS-SAFE-001 paper-engine HALTED gate sub-component (SyRS SYS-44a; NFR-P3; NFR-SC1; StRS SN-1.11). The kill switch's acceptance criterion includes: 'paper simulation engines transition to the HALTED state with no further on_fill callbacks emitted'. This slice ships ONE named sub-component of that clause: the per-engine Running -> Halted transition and the refuse-to-fill gate, in crates/atp-simulation (module `halt`), std-only and in-crate (no new dependency). DOMAIN-LEVEL REALIZATION (honest scope): there is no callback-emitting runtime loop and no Python strategy host yet, so 'no further on_fill callbacks emitted' is realized at the domain level -- a HALTED HaltablePaperEngine refuses to PRODUCE a sim::PaperFill (HaltablePaperEngine::simulate_fill returns HaltError::Halted with no fill value), so no fill exists to drive an on_fill callback. The inner sim::PaperSimulationEngine::simulate_fill stays a pure, stateless, deterministic cost path and is NOT modified. SEALED GATE (and its honest limit): HaltablePaperEngine owns a PRIVATE PaperSimulationEngine (the `engine` field is not pub, there is no accessor returning &PaperSimulationEngine, no Deref, no into_inner) AND the gate is not Clone (a clone would be an independent value whose own state could stay Running after the original is halted, so a pre-halt copy could keep filling -- the exact bypass the gate prevents). So for a given gate VALUE the only path to a paper fill is the gate's own simulate_fill, which matches on the run state first: PaperEngineState::Halted returns HaltError::Halted BEFORE touching the inner engine, while PaperEngineState::Running delegates to self.engine.simulate_fill and maps a fill-native SimError to HaltError::Sim UNCHANGED (the gate never masks a fill error while Running). This seals a HELD gate, NOT the whole system: the bare PaperSimulationEngine stays a public fill primitive (backtests, the SRS-SIM-001/002/003 operator CLIs, and the SRS-SIM-003 virtual-ledger path construct and drive it directly), so a caller can still build a bare engine and fill OUTSIDE the gate; guaranteeing every non-live strategy is HOSTED on a halt-aware engine (so a kill switch reaches all of them) is the Strategy Orchestrator's routing job (SRS-EXE-002), deferred -- this slice provides the per-engine sealed-halt primitive that orchestrator will compose. simulate_fill is &self (it only READS state to decide), so a fill never mutates the engine and the path stays deterministic. IDEMPOTENT HALT: halt(reason) is &mut self; the first call flips the state to Halted, records a HaltTransition with a monotonic sequence (starts at 1; no wall-clock time), and returns HaltOutcome::Transitioned; any later call is a no-op returning HaltOutcome::AlreadyHalted that leaves the state Halted and the recorded transition (sequence + reason) untouched. HaltReason is intentionally a closed set (KillSwitch only -- no speculative variants). HaltTransition (reason + sequence) is the in-memory groundwork for SRS-LOG-001 observability and carries NO wall-clock time, so it makes no latency claim. HaltError composes the fill-native SimError via `impl From<SimError> for HaltError`, exactly as SimError composes CostError. The Rust integration test srs_safe_001_paper_halt drives the gate end to end (running fills identically to the bare engine; a halted engine produces no fill for buy/sell/spread orders; halt is idempotent; the transition is observable; the gate does not mask fill-native errors; the same order fills then is refused across the transition; determinism across identical sequences). The PASS line is 'SRS-SAFE-001 HALT-GATE PASS' -- a SUB-COMPONENT pass, NOT a full/SDK-SURFACE pass. feature_list.json keeps SRS-SAFE-001 passes:false: this is one named sub-component and the full QuantConnect-Liquidate sequence is genuinely unbuilt and owned elsewhere (see deferred[]).

It does NOT make every paper fill in the system flow through the gate: the bare PaperSimulationEngine stays a public fill primitive used by backtests and the SRS-SIM-001/002/003 CLIs, so a caller can fill outside the gate. sim_persistence_contract.description: SRS-SIM-004 (persist paper strategy simulation state; SyRS SYS-89; StRS SN-1.29 / SN-2.05). The internal simulation engine's paper-state persistence path lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `paper_state`) and consumes the SRS-SIM-003 VirtualLedgerBook without any new dependency (no serde): money stays integer minor units (i64 quantity, i128 money) with no f64. A PaperStateSnapshot is a versioned envelope holding schema_version (i64), the PersistenceConfig in force, and a clone of the VirtualLedgerBook. SYS-89 names four sub-states (virtual position ledger, pending simulated orders, accumulated metrics, and the user-state dictionary), but only the virtual ledger has a runtime type today, so the snapshot captures the FULL ledger (every strategy, every symbol, all six VirtualPosition fields) and reserves three forward-compatible, always-empty slots for the other three sub-states; deserialize fails closed (PersistenceError::UnsupportedSection) on a non-zero reserved-slot count rather than silently dropping data a v1 reader has no type to restore. PersistenceConfig carries the SYS-89 cadence: interval_secs (u64, default DEFAULT_INTERVAL_SECS = 60), restore_deadline_secs (u64, default DEFAULT_RESTORE_DEADLINE_SECS = 30), and persist_on_shutdown (bool). PersistenceConfig::new takes only the interval and the restore deadline (NOT a shutdown flag) and fails closed on a cadence that cannot meet SYS-89: a zero-second interval or restore deadline (PersistenceError::NonPositiveConfig), and a restore deadline ABOVE the 30s ceiling (PersistenceError::RestoreDeadlineTooLong), so the config CANNOT encode an SLA slower than the 30 seconds the requirement mandates (an operator may only tighten it). The persist interval has no upper ceiling because SYS-89 makes 60s the default, not a maximum, so a longer cadence is a permitted operator trade-off. Shutdown persistence is MANDATORY: SYS-89 requires state to be persisted at the interval AND on container shutdown, so persist_on_shutdown is always true and the caller cannot disable it; a snapshot that encodes shutdown persistence as disabled is out of contract and rejected on restore (PersistenceError::ShutdownPersistenceRequired). The serializer is DETERMINISTIC: HashMap iteration order is unspecified, so serialize sorts strategies by id (StrategyId is not Ord, so by as_str()) and positions by canonical symbol before emitting, so the same state always serializes to byte-identical output (an unchanged 60s checkpoint never churns). The codec is hand-rolled and dependency-free: integers are decimal lines and variable-length strings are length-prefixed (value.len().to_string()), so a canonical symbol containing spaces (an OCC option contract such as 'AAPL  240119C00190000') round-trips losslessly. The serialized frame is the MAGIC line, a 64-bit FNV-1a integrity checksum over the body, then the body; deserialize verifies the checksum BEFORE building any state, so a structurally-valid byte change (a tampered-but-sign-consistent quantity/basis pair, a flipped digit, truncation, or appended bytes) fails closed with PersistenceError::ChecksumMismatch under fault injection rather than restoring fabricated positions or P&L. The checksum is a non-cryptographic integrity guard for ACCIDENTAL corruption (the SYS-89 fault-injection criterion), not a security MAC -- defending against a deliberate tamperer who can recompute the checksum needs a keyed MAC and key management, which is out of scope for the single-user, local-only release baseline (no multi-user auth per the system constraints). capture() builds a snapshot at SCHEMA_VERSION; serialize() emits the text; deserialize() parses it back; restore() is the convenience round-trip restore(serialized) = PaperStateSnapshot::deserialize(serialized).map(PaperStateSnapshot::into_book), so restore(serialize(capture(book))) == book exactly (quantity, signed cost basis, realized P&L, and every cost component survive, including a FLAT fully-closed position that still carries realized P&L and commission). deserialize is fail-closed and atomic: it validates the magic header (MAGIC = 'ATP-PAPER-STATE') and the schema version (a foreign blob or unknown version is rejected, not mis-read: PersistenceError::CorruptSnapshot / UnknownSchemaVersion), validates the config, validates every position field invariant BEFORE the position is built (cost components non-negative; the quantity/basis biconditional (quantity == 0) iff (cost_basis_minor == 0); sign agreement between a non-flat quantity and its basis; a canonical, non-empty symbol; a non-empty strategy id; no duplicate strategy id or duplicate symbol within a strategy: PersistenceError::InconsistentField / DuplicateRecord), and requires the cursor to be exhausted at the end (trailing bytes are corrupt). It builds the whole VirtualLedgerBook in a local and returns it only on full success, so a corrupt, truncated, or tampered snapshot returns an Err and yields NO partially-restored book (never a phantom strategy or symbol). paper_state adds no broker/adapter/live-position dependency and carries no vendor SDK token, so persisted paper state is structurally independent of the IB account (the SYS-84 isolation property holds through persistence too). The Rust integration test srs_sim_004_paper_state drives capture/serialize/restore end to end from real simulate_fill output. The PASS line is 'SRS-SIM-004 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-004 passes:false because the live 60s persistence timer firing inside a running container and a real restart restoring within 30s need the SRS-EXE-002 orchestrator wiring and the SYS-89 container lifecycle, the pending_orders slot needs a paper-order pending store (SRS-SIM-001 / SRS-SIM-002 own no such store yet), the metrics slot needs the SYS-85 / SRS-BT-004 paper metric family, and the user_state slot needs the Python strategy runtime.

deferred.feature: Live 60s persistence timer and 30s-restore on a real container restart
deferred.what: SYS-89 requires the state to be persisted every 60 seconds (and on shutdown) and restored within 30 seconds of a container restart. This slice ships the deterministic capture/serialize/restore codec and the cadence config, but the live timer firing inside a running container and the restart that reads the blob back need the SRS-EXE-002 orchestrator wiring and the SYS-89 container lifecycle, which own the strategy container and its restart, so the timing guarantees are deferred to that owner and feature_list.json keeps SRS-SIM-004 passes:false.

SYS-89 lists pending simulated orders among the persisted sub-states, but the SRS-SIM-001 / SRS-SIM-002 paper-order path only accepts and routes orders; nothing holds an accepted-but-unfilled order as runtime state, so there is no pending-order store to persist. The snapshot reserves a forward-compatible pending_orders slot (always empty in v1, fail-closed on non-empty); populating it needs a paper-order pending store owned by the SRS-SIM-001 / SRS-SIM-002 fill path.

SYS-89 lists accumulated performance metrics among the persisted sub-states, but no PaperMetrics type exists yet (the SYS-85 / SRS-BT-004 metric family computes returns, drawdown, and ratios over the ledger's P&L primitives and is its own feature). The snapshot reserves a forward-compatible metrics slot (always empty in v1, fail-closed on non-empty); populating it needs the SYS-85 / SRS-BT-004 metric family.

SYS-89 lists the user-state dictionary (the JSON-serializable key-value state a user strategy writes via the strategy API) among the persisted sub-states, but that dictionary is produced by the Python strategy runtime, which is not built yet. The snapshot reserves a forward-compatible user_state slot (always empty in v1, fail-closed on non-empty); populating it needs the SRS-SDK Python strategy host.

sim_metrics_contract.description: SRS-BT-004 (compute required backtest and paper/live performance metrics; SyRS SYS-16, SYS-86; StRS SN-1.04 / SN-1.05 / SN-1.29). The shared performance-metric family lives in crates/atp-simulation (module `metrics`) and computes the eight SYS-16 metrics -- Sharpe ratio, Sortino ratio, alpha, beta, maximum drawdown, annualized return, annualized volatility, and win rate -- for one completed run. SYS-86 requires the internal simulation engine to compute the SAME family for paper strategies that the backtest engine and the live dashboard compute, so the module consumes the primitives the engine already produces: a mark-to-market backtest::EquityPoint curve and a backtest::Fill trade log, both in integer minor units. The headline design decision is the numeric boundary: money enters in integer minor units (EquityPoint.equity_minor: i64, BenchmarkPoint.level_minor: i64, every Fill money field i64), but the metrics themselves are dimensionless f64 ratios -- so unlike paper_state this module DOES contain f64, which is the metric domain, not a money-correctness leak. The f64 work is made deterministic (the SRS-BT-010 criterion this family must honor): every reduction is a fixed left-to-right fold over the timestamp-ordered series, with no parallelism, no platform RNG, and no wall-clock read, so identical inputs always produce bit-identical metrics; the deserializer rejects a non-strictly-increasing timestamp (MetricsError::NonMonotonicTimestamps) so the period returns are never order-dependent. A metric that is mathematically undefined on the given input -- Sharpe/Sortino/volatility when there are fewer than two returns or zero dispersion, alpha/beta when no benchmark series was supplied or the benchmark has zero variance, win rate when no trade closed -- is reported as None, never as a fabricated 0.0 and never as a leaked NaN/inf; every computed metric is verified finite before it is returned (MetricsError::NonFiniteComputation), so a pathological input fails closed rather than emitting a poison value. Period returns require a strictly positive equity curve (MetricsError::NonPositiveEquity) because a return divides by the prior equity. Alpha and beta are defined only against a benchmark (SYS-16 computes them relative to the SYS-17 benchmark): Benchmark defaults to SPY (DEFAULT_BENCHMARK_SYMBOL) when the operator selects none and the chosen symbol is echoed on PerformanceMetrics.benchmark_symbol so a report identifies its benchmark; the caller-supplied benchmark level series must align 1:1 by timestamp with the equity curve (MetricsError::BenchmarkLengthMismatch / BenchmarkTimestampMismatch) and be strictly positive (MetricsError::NonPositiveBenchmarkLevel). The win rate is the fraction of COMPLETE round trips whose NET P&L was strictly positive. A trade is a round trip -- the flat-to-flat span over which a symbol's position leaves zero, through any number of fills, and returns to zero -- not an individual fill, so the win rate is INVARIANT to fill fragmentation: a position closed in one fill versus three volume-capped partial fills (SRS-SIM-002) is the SAME one winning/losing round trip, keeping backtest (aggregated) and paper/live (fragmented) win rates comparable (SYS-86). A round trip's net P&L is its realized cash flow minus all its transaction costs (net = -sum(signed_qty_i * price_i) - sum(cost_i) over the round trip's fills, via closing_cash_flow); because both are sums over the whole flat-to-flat span, the result is INVARIANT to the order of fills within the round trip, so it cannot silently depend on intra-bar fill ordering, and a win counts only when that NET P&L is strictly positive (a gross profit overwhelmed by costs is correctly a loss). A single fill that REVERSES through zero settles the closed round trip and opens the next; because there is no fragmentation-invariant way to split one fill's integer cost across the two round trips it spans, the reversing fill's FULL cost is deterministically attributed to the closing round trip (equivalent to writing the reversal as a close-to-flat fill with the cost on the close, then a zero-cost open), which avoids the integer-floor bias that would charge a small reversing close zero cost and record a spurious win. Symbols are canonicalized with the ledger's canonical_symbol policy (trim + upper-case) so an open on AAPL and a close on aapl close the SAME round trip (SYS-86 parity); a negative cost component is rejected (MetricsError::NegativeFillCost), a non-positive fill price is rejected (MetricsError::NonPositiveFillPrice), and the i128 money math is checked (MetricsError::Overflow). The trade log must be time-ordered: a backwards timestamp is rejected (MetricsError::NonMonotonicTradeLog), but MULTIPLE fills may share a timestamp (several orders, or volume-capped partial fills, can fill against one bar) and are applied in TRADE-LOG (slice) ORDER -- the producer's authoritative execution sequence -- so a legitimate multi-fill bar is processed in order rather than rejected, and the result is deterministic for a given trade log. The trade log and the equity curve must describe the SAME run: every fill timestamp must fall within the equity curve's observed window [first mark ts, last mark ts] or it is rejected as incoherent (MetricsError::TradeLogOutsideRun), catching a stale or mismatched (e.g. asynchronously-snapshotted later) trade log paired with a different run's equity curve so the win rate and the equity-derived metrics cannot silently disagree. The FULL atomic run-snapshot identity (a run id binding the equity curve, trade log, and metrics into one consistent snapshot) is the deferred paper/live accumulator's responsibility; this window check is the in-scope coherence guard. The pre-trade baseline is a REQUIRED compute input (starting_equity_minor: i64), NOT an implicit first curve point, so the first period's P&L (including opening-fill entry costs) can never be silently omitted: period returns are [(equity_curve[0] - starting) / starting, ...], the drawdown peak starts at the baseline (let mut peak = starting_equity_minor as f64), and annualized return runs from the baseline (let first = starting_equity_minor as f64). Producers pass their starting cash here (the backtest engine passes BacktestResult's request.starting_cash_minor), so the metric never guesses a baseline. The baseline and every NON-final mark must be strictly positive (each is the denominator of a return), but the FINAL mark may be exactly zero -- a total loss / bankruptcy is a defined -100% final return (and a 100% drawdown), so it is computed, not rejected; only a non-final non-positive mark or a negative final mark is rejected (MetricsError::NonPositiveEquity), so one bad mark never aborts an otherwise-valid run. The benchmark's pre-trade baseline is the FIRST element of benchmark_levels (its remaining points align 1:1 by timestamp with equity_curve, so benchmark_levels has equity_curve.len() + 1 entries and yields the same number of returns as the strategy). Beta is cov(strategy, benchmark) / var(benchmark); Jensen's alpha is computed on EXCESS returns over the per-period risk-free rate ((mean_strategy - rf) - beta * (mean_benchmark - rf), annualized arithmetically), since omitting the risk-free rate is only correct when rf == 0 or beta == 1. Sharpe, Sortino, and beta divide by a dispersion; rather than an exact == 0.0 denominator check (which floating-point aggregation of near-identical returns can slip past, dividing by ~1e-17 to produce an enormous but finite, meaningless ratio), a scale-aware tolerance (negligible_dispersion / DISPERSION_EPSILON, relative to the largest absolute observation) treats a noise-level standard deviation, downside deviation, or benchmark variance as zero so a near-degenerate series yields None rather than a spurious ranking-corrupting value. MetricsConfig carries the annualization factor (periods_per_year: u32, default DEFAULT_PERIODS_PER_YEAR = 252) and the per-period risk-free rate (risk_free_rate_per_period: f64, default 0.0); MetricsConfig::new fails closed on a zero annualization factor (MetricsError::NonPositivePeriodsPerYear) and a non-finite or <= -100%/period risk-free rate (MetricsError::InvalidRiskFreeRate). metrics adds no broker/adapter/live dependency and carries no vendor SDK token, so the family is structurally independent of the IB account; lib.rs re-exports `pub mod metrics;`. The Rust integration test srs_bt_004_metrics drives compute() end to end from a real BacktestResult. The PASS line is 'SRS-BT-004 SDK-SURFACE PASS'; feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-004 passes:false because the live dashboard reporting path and the paper/live runtime accumulators that feed this family at runtime (the SRS-SIM-004 snapshot reserves the metrics slot for them, still empty until an accumulator exists), and the SRS-BT-005 benchmark-resolution surface (resolving SPY's actual price history and wiring the dashboard/report benchmark identification), are deferred owners.

baseline.drawdown_peak_token: let mut peak = starting_equity_minor as f64

paper_accumulator.description: SYS-86 requires the internal simulation engine to compute the SAME metric family for paper strategies that the backtest engine computes. crates/atp-simulation/src/paper_metrics.rs is the paper-side PRODUCER of the two primitives metrics::compute consumes: PaperMetricsAccumulator accumulates the mark-to-market net-liquidation equity curve (cash + sum(position * mark), the identical quantity the backtest engine marks via cash + position * close) and the trade log from the SYS-84 virtual ledger (StrategyLedger) and the simulated PaperFill stream, then delegates to the shared metrics::compute on compute_metrics(). market_value_minor on VirtualPosition (virtual_ledger.rs) is the net-liq building block (mark * quantity, == cost_basis + unrealized_pnl, fail-closed on a non-positive mark). The accumulator fails closed on a missing mark for an open position (MissingMark -- never value a held position at zero), a duplicate symbol within one mark instant (DuplicateMark), a non-strictly-increasing mark timestamp (NonMonotonicMarkTimestamps) or a backwards fill (NonMonotonicFill), and a non-positive starting cash baseline (NonPositiveStartingCash). It also enforces CROSS-STREAM chronological coherence between the fill and mark streams: a fill at or before an already-recorded mark is rejected (FillBeforeMark -- it would retroactively change a position the curve has already valued), and a mark earlier than the latest applied fill is rejected (MarkBeforeFill -- it would value a past instant using a future position), so an out-of-order interleaving cannot fabricate a time-incoherent equity curve that metrics::compute (seeing only a monotonic curve and an in-window fill) could not detect; the legitimate within-bar apply-fills-then-mark order (equal timestamps) is permitted. It reads no clock/RNG so it is deterministic. The srs_bt_004_metrics integration test drives a real BacktestEngine and this accumulator from the same activity and asserts the metric families are EQUAL (srs_bt_004_paper_metrics_match_the_backtest_family, plus a with-costs variant). The sim_paper_metrics_cli binary renders the eight paper metrics and a parity subcommand that prints paper-backtest-parity:true only when every metric matches. This closes the in-scope paper-accumulator half; the live runtime that SUPPLIES the marks (SYS-70 feed) and the dashboard/persisted-slot wiring stay deferred, so SRS-BT-004 keeps passes:false.

deferred.what: SYS-16 / SYS-86 require these metrics to be reported for completed backtests, paper strategies, AND the live dashboard. This slice ships the deterministic computation, but the dashboard/API path that surfaces them at a <= 5s refresh (SYS-36) is the SRS-UI / SRS-API owner, so the live-reporting half is deferred and feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-004 passes:false.

deferred.what: The deterministic, dependency-free paper-side accumulator now EXISTS (paper_accumulator above: PaperMetricsAccumulator in paper_metrics.rs), so a paper strategy computes the SAME metric family the backtest engine does (proven equal in the srs_bt_004_metrics integration test, demonstrable over fixtures via sim_paper_metrics_cli). What remains deferred is the RUNTIME that supplies the accumulator's marks at production time -- marking each open position against the live/paper market-data feed (SYS-70 subscription manager) on the strategy's cadence -- and the wiring that feeds the accumulated family into the SRS-SIM-004 persisted metrics slot (still empty until that runtime populates it) and onto the live dashboard. Those owners are the paper/live execution runtime and SRS-UI / SRS-API, so the end-to-end runtime half stays deferred and feature_list.json keeps SRS-BT-004 passes:false.

The backtest path passes BacktestResult's request.starting_cash_minor; the deferred paper/live accumulators will likewise pass their own starting equity. Resolving and threading that starting equity from each runtime mode is the producer's responsibility; this slice fully handles the baseline at the metric boundary and tests it (a single-mark curve still has a first period from the baseline, and an initial drawdown below starting equity is captured).
